input.assessment: Acute exacerbation of COPD, likely infectious etiology given productive sputum and fever. Community-acquired pneumonia cannot be ruled out. Patient's symptoms consistent with GOLD criteria for moderate-severe exacerbation requiring hospital admission.
input.plan: Admit to medical floor for acute COPD exacerbation management. Start prednisone 40mg PO daily x 5 days, azithromycin 500mg PO day 1 then 250mg daily x 4 days, continue nebulized albuterol/ipratropium q4h, supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 >90%, pulmonary hygiene, incentive spirometry. Chest X-ray, CBC, BMP, sputum culture. Restart maintenance inhalers: fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 BID. Smoking cessation counseling. Social work consult for medication assistance program.
recommendations: Complete 5-day steroid course and 5-day antibiotic course
Follow up with pulmonology in 2 weeks after discharge
Ensure patient enrolled in medication assistance program before discharge
Pulmonary rehabilitation referral
Annual influenza vaccine and pneumococcal vaccine if not up to date
input.results: Chest X-ray: Right lower lobe infiltrate consistent with pneumonia vs atelectasis. CBC: WBC 14.2 (elevated, left shift), Hgb 13.5. BMP: Normal. SpO2 88% on room air, improved to 94% on 2L O2 via nasal cannula.
input.notes: Patient presents with classic COPD exacerbation triggered by respiratory infection. Risk factors include former heavy smoking history, poor medication adherence (ran out of maintenance inhalers), and baseline severe COPD. This is patient's 3rd exacerbation requiring hospitalization in past 12 months, indicating high exacerbation risk phenotype. Social determinants of health: medication affordability barriers (ran out of inhalers), may benefit from patient assistance programs. Patient motivated to avoid future hospitalizations, receptive to pulmonary rehab.

input.type: Annual Wellness Visit - Preventive Care Screening Review
input.provider: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD - Primary Care
input.findings: Patient is 65-year-old with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and obesity. Due for multiple preventive screenings: Last dental cleaning 8 months ago (overdue for 6-month interval), last eye exam 14 months ago (annual diabetic retinopathy screening overdue), last foot exam 3 months ago (compliant with quarterly diabetic foot exam), no prior skin cancer screening documented, no formal self-exam education provided, hearing screening not performed in past 5 years, bone density scan never completed despite age >65 and diabetes risk factors.
input.assessment: Multiple gaps in preventive care screening identified. Patient at high risk for complications: Diabetic retinopathy risk due to 10-year diabetes duration, peripheral neuropathy risk requiring regular foot exams, increased skin cancer risk (fair skin, outdoor occupation), age-appropriate hearing and bone health screening needed. Current screening adherence suboptimal - dental and eye exams overdue.
input.plan: Comprehensive preventive care screening plan: 1) Schedule diabetic eye exam within 2 weeks 2) Dental referral for cleaning and periodontal evaluation 3) Continue quarterly diabetic foot exams 4) Dermatology referral for full-body skin cancer screening 5) Provide breast/testicular self-exam education materials 6) Audiology referral for baseline hearing test 7) Order DEXA scan for osteoporosis screening 8) Patient education on importance of preventive care adherence

recommendations.recommendation: Schedule dental cleaning every 6 months, periodontal evaluation for diabetes-related gum disease
Annual diabetic retinopathy screening with dilated fundoscopic exam by ophthalmology
Quarterly diabetic foot exams with monofilament testing, annual vascular assessment
Full-body skin exam by dermatology, annual screening recommended due to fair skin and sun exposure
DEXA scan for osteoporosis screening, repeat every 2 years if normal

input.results: Patient verbalized understanding of preventive care screening gaps. Motivated to schedule overdue appointments. Concerned about cost of multiple specialist visits - provided information on insurance coverage for preventive services. Eye exam appointment scheduled for 12/5/24. Dental office contact information provided. Self-exam education materials accepted.
input.status: Active Preventive Care Plan - Multiple Screenings Scheduled
input.dentalCleaning: Last cleaning: 3/15/2024 (8 months ago). Overdue for 6-month interval cleaning. Dental referral placed for cleaning and periodontal evaluation - diabetes increases gum disease risk. Recommend cleaning every 6 months. Patient reports occasional gum bleeding with flossing. Dental office: Dr. Miller, contact provided. Appointment needed within 1 month.
input.eyeExam: Last dilated eye exam: 9/20/2023 (14 months ago). OVERDUE for annual diabetic retinopathy screening. Patient has 10-year diabetes history - high risk for retinopathy. Eye exam appointment scheduled with Dr. Johnson, Ophthalmology for 12/5/2024. Annual dilated fundoscopic exam required for diabetes. Previous exam showed no retinopathy, but annual surveillance critical. Will obtain records from ophthalmology after visit.
input.footExam: Last diabetic foot exam: 8/20/2024 (3 months ago) - COMPLIANT with quarterly screening. Monofilament testing: Intact protective sensation bilaterally. No ulcers, calluses, or deformities noted. Pedal pulses 2+ bilaterally. Patient demonstrates good foot hygiene, inspects feet daily, wears appropriate footwear. Next foot exam due: 2/20/2025 (quarterly intervals). Continue current foot care regimen. Patient educated on daily foot inspection and proper nail trimming.
input.skinCancerScreening: NO PRIOR skin cancer screening documented. Patient is 65-year-old with fair skin (Fitzpatrick Type II), outdoor occupation (landscaper for 30 years) - HIGH RISK for skin cancer. Dermatology referral placed for full-body skin exam. Discussed ABCDE warning signs (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6mm, Evolving). Patient to perform monthly self-skin exams. Annual dermatology screening recommended. Appointment pending scheduling.
input.selfExamEducation: Provided comprehensive self-examination education today: 1) Breast self-exam (for female patients) or testicular self-exam (for male patients) - monthly examination technique reviewed with printed materials and demonstration 2) Skin self-exam - monthly full-body skin check for new or changing moles, provided ABCDE criteria handout and mirror for back examination 3) Oral self-exam - monthly check for lesions, white patches, persistent sores. Patient demonstrated understanding, accepted all educational materials. Will reinforce at next visit.
input.hearingTest: NO hearing screening in past 5 years. Patient is 65 years old - age-appropriate screening recommended. Patient reports occasional difficulty hearing in noisy environments, asks for repetition frequently (noticed during visit). Audiology referral placed for baseline audiometry. Hearing loss screening recommended every 1-3 years for adults >65. Early detection important for cognitive health and fall prevention. Appointment pending - audiology will contact patient.

input.provider: Dr. Michael Chen, MD - Family Medicine

recommendations.recommendation: Continue current diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and asthma medications. Enroll in medication assistance programs to improve adherence. 3-month follow-up for monitoring.
URGENT: Schedule colonoscopy (10 years overdue, family history of polyps). Schedule diabetic eye exam (overdue 6 months).
Administer 2024 influenza vaccine and Shingrix dose 2 today. Up-to-date after today's administration.
Grief counseling resources provided. Senior center enrollment for social engagement. PHQ-9 follow-up in 1 month.
Social work consult for medication assistance, transportation programs, and meal delivery services. Address financial and social barriers to care.

input.status: Active Comprehensive Care Plan - Chronic Disease Management + Preventive Care + Social Support

diabetes.medications: Metformin 1000mg PO BID, Insulin glargine (Lantus) 20 units subcutaneous QHS
diabetes.medicationAdherence: Suboptimal - patient admits to skipping metformin doses to stretch supply due to cost ($45/month copay on fixed income). Insulin adherence better (higher priority in patient's mind). TODAY: Enrolled in manufacturer assistance program - metformin now FREE.
diabetes.complications: None clinically evident. Screening: No retinopathy (last exam 5/2023, overdue for repeat), no nephropathy (uACR <30 mg/g 4/2024), no neuropathy (monofilament test normal today, intact sensation), no diabetic foot ulcers.
diabetes.complicationsScreening: Retinopathy: Overdue (schedule ophthalmology). Nephropathy: Current (uACR due in 3 months). Neuropathy: Current (foot exam today normal). Cardiovascular: On statin, BP controlled, aspirin not indicated (USPSTF recommends against primary prevention in most adults).
diabetes.selfMonitoring: Fasting blood glucose checked 3x/week at home. Range: 110-145 mg/dL (acceptable for older adult). Patient has glucometer, adequate strips. Educated on target range (80-130 fasting, <180 postprandial).
diabetes.dietAdherence: Moderate. Patient attempts carbohydrate counting, limits sweets, but food insecurity affects choices (relies on food bank - limited fresh produce, high processed carbs). Referred to Meals on Wheels for diabetic-appropriate meals.
diabetes.exercisePattern: Minimal. Walks to mailbox daily (100 feet), limited by knee osteoarthritis. Goal: Increase to 10-minute walks 3x/week. Physical therapy referral for safe exercise plan.
diabetes.patientEducation: Reviewed: importance of medication adherence (A1c control prevents complications), foot care (daily inspection, proper footwear), sick day management (never skip insulin), hypoglycemia recognition and treatment (glucose tabs at home), annual eye exams (prevent blindness), nephropathy screening. Patient demonstrates good understanding.
diabetes.goals: Maintain HbA1c <7.5%, prevent hypoglycemia (especially important on insulin), screen for/prevent complications, improve medication adherence via financial assistance, increase physical activity safely
diabetes.nextSteps: 3-month follow-up: Repeat HbA1c, assess medication adherence post-assistance enrollment, check BP, order uACR + creatinine. 1-year: Repeat comprehensive metabolic panel, lipid panel. Ongoing: Annual dilated eye exam, annual foot exam, annual uACR.

hypertension.bloodPressureTrend: Consistently 125-135 / 75-82 over past 6 months (home monitoring log reviewed)
hypertension.medications: Lisinopril 10mg PO daily (ACE inhibitor - preferred for diabetes + HTN), Amlodipine 5mg PO daily (calcium channel blocker, added 2020 for better control)
hypertension.medicationAdherence: Good - patient prioritizes BP meds (fears stroke after father had CVA). No skipped doses. Enrolled in assistance program today - copays reduced.
hypertension.complications: No target organ damage: No history of MI, stroke, heart failure, or renal dysfunction. Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (normal), eGFR >60 (normal kidney function). No left ventricular hypertrophy on last ECG (2023).
hypertension.selfMonitoring: Home BP monitoring with automatic cuff. Checks BP 2x/week, maintains log (reviewed today). Patient demonstrates correct technique. Values correlate with office readings (no white coat hypertension).
hypertension.lifestyleModifications: DASH diet attempted but limited by food access (food bank items high sodium). Sodium intake estimated 3000-3500 mg/day (goal <2300 mg). Low physical activity (limited by knee pain). Weight stable (BMI 29 - overweight). Discussed low-sodium food choices within food bank constraints.
hypertension.cardiovascularRiskReduction: On aspirin 81mg daily for primary prevention (STOPPED per USPSTF 2022 - bleeding risk outweighs benefit). On statin (atorvastatin 40mg) for hyperlipidemia. Diabetes + HTN = high CV risk - aggressive risk factor management.
hypertension.patientEducation: Reviewed: medication adherence importance (prevent stroke, MI, kidney disease), home BP monitoring technique, low-sodium food swaps, warning signs of hypertensive emergency (severe headache, chest pain, vision changes, shortness of breath - call 911), importance of regular follow-up.
hypertension.goals: Maintain BP <130/80, prevent cardiovascular events, preserve kidney function, optimize medication adherence
hypertension.nextSteps: 3-month follow-up: BP check, medication adherence assessment, home BP log review. Annual: ECG, creatinine/eGFR, uACR (proteinuria screening). Continue home BP monitoring 2x/week.

hyperlipidemia.lipidPanel: LDL 92 mg/dL, HDL 48 mg/dL, Total cholesterol 185 mg/dL, Triglycerides 145 mg/dL (6/2024)
hyperlipidemia.ldlGoal: <100 mg/dL (diabetes = high ASCVD risk, some guidelines suggest <70 for very high risk)
hyperlipidemia.ldlTrend: Improved since starting statin: Baseline LDL 165 → Current 92 mg/dL (44% reduction)
hyperlipidemia.ascvdRiskScore: 15.2% 10-year risk (diabetes, age 62, HTN, current smoking status NO, HDL 48, BP 128/78) - HIGH RISK
hyperlipidemia.medications: Atorvastatin 40mg PO QHS (high-intensity statin for diabetes + high ASCVD risk)
hyperlipidemia.medicationAdherence: Good - patient understands statin prevents heart attack/stroke. Enrolled in assistance program today - copay eliminated.
hyperlipidemia.statinSideEffects: No myalgias, no muscle weakness, no liver enzyme elevation. CK checked 2023 (normal). Patient tolerates statin well.
hyperlipidemia.lifestyleModifications: Low saturated fat diet attempted (limited by food access). Minimal trans fats. Patient avoids fried foods. Increased fiber intake (oatmeal for breakfast when available). Exercise limited by knee pain (PT referral to address barrier).
hyperlipidemia.patientEducation: Reviewed: statin importance for diabetes patients (major CV risk reduction even with borderline LDL), dietary cholesterol vs saturated fat (focus on sat fat reduction), exercise benefits (raises HDL), warning signs of statin myopathy (muscle pain, weakness, dark urine - report immediately).
hyperlipidemia.goals: Maintain LDL <100 mg/dL, raise HDL >50 mg/dL (low HDL is residual risk), triglycerides <150 mg/dL, prevent ASCVD events (MI, stroke)
hyperlipidemia.nextSteps: Annual lipid panel (due 6/2025). Monitor for statin side effects. Continue atorvastatin 40mg. If LDL rises above goal, consider increasing to atorvastatin 80mg or adding ezetimibe.

asthma.severity: Mild persistent (symptoms 2-6 days/week but not daily, nighttime awakenings 3-4x/month)
asthma.controlAssessment: Well-controlled: No daytime symptoms in past 4 weeks, no nighttime awakenings, no activity limitation, no rescue inhaler use in past month, no exacerbations in past year, PEF >80% predicted.
asthma.medications: Fluticasone/Salmeterol (Advair) 250/50 mcg - 1 puff inhaled BID (ICS/LABA combination for persistent asthma). Albuterol MDI 90mcg - 2 puffs Q4-6H PRN for acute symptoms (rescue inhaler).
asthma.medicationAdherence: Excellent for controller (fluticasone/salmeterol) - patient uses daily as prescribed, understands this is prevention. Rescue inhaler (albuterol) rarely needed - last use 3 months ago (URI trigger). Enrolled in assistance program - both inhalers copay reduced.
asthma.peakFlow: Personal best 380 L/min. Today's PEF: 360 L/min (95% of personal best - excellent). Green zone (>80% personal best) consistently.
asthma.triggers: Upper respiratory infections (viral URIs trigger bronchospasm - last exacerbation 14 months ago). Cold air (mild trigger - uses scarf over mouth in winter). No allergic triggers identified (no seasonal pattern, no pet/dust mite exacerbation). Tobacco smoke (never smoker, avoids secondhand smoke).
asthma.exacerbationHistory: Last exacerbation: 10/2023 (14 months ago) - viral URI triggered wheezing, increased albuterol use, managed at home with prednisone course (40mg x 5 days). No ED visits, no hospitalizations in past 5 years. No ICU admissions ever.
asthma.inhalerTechnique: Assessed today - CORRECT technique for both MDIs. Patient uses spacer with fluticasone/salmeterol (improves deposition, reduces oral thrush risk). Rinses mouth after ICS use (prevents thrush). Shakes inhaler, exhales fully, slow deep inhalation, holds breath 10 seconds.
asthma.patientEducation: Reviewed: difference between controller (daily prevention) and rescue (acute relief) inhalers, asthma action plan (green/yellow/red zones based on symptoms + PEF), early intervention for URI (start increasing controller, use rescue for symptoms, call if worsening), trigger avoidance, when to seek emergency care (severe SOB, chest tightness, PEF <50% personal best, no improvement with albuterol).
asthma.asthmaActionPlan: Green zone (doing well): Current status. Continue fluticasone/salmeterol BID, albuterol PRN rarely. Yellow zone (getting worse): Symptoms increase, PEF 50-80% personal best, albuterol needed >2x/week. Action: Increase fluticasone/salmeterol to TID, use albuterol Q4H, call clinic. Red zone (medical alert): Severe SOB, PEF <50%, albuterol not helping. Action: Take 4 puffs albuterol immediately, call 911 or go to ED, may need prednisone/hospitalization.
asthma.goals: Maintain asthma control (no symptoms, no exacerbations, no activity limitation), prevent exacerbations requiring steroids/ED visits, optimize inhaler technique, avoid triggers
asthma.nextSteps: Annual asthma review (due 11/2025). Continue current regimen (well-controlled). PEF monitoring at home if symptoms develop. Spirometry if control deteriorates (not needed now - clinically stable). Flu vaccine administered today (prevents URI triggers). Consider pneumococcal vaccine at age 65 (chronic lung disease).

getCardiovascularRiskReduction.description: Retrieve cardiovascular risk reduction records for a patient

input.type: Comprehensive Cardiovascular Risk Reduction Plan - Primary Prevention + Chronic Disease Management
input.provider: Dr. Michael Rodriguez, MD - Cardiology & Dr. Sarah Chen, MD - Primary Care
input.facility: Memorial Hospital Cardiovascular Center + Primary Care Clinic
input.assessment:
HIGH-RISK CARDIOVASCULAR PATIENT requiring AGGRESSIVE multi-factorial risk reduction:

1️⃣ LIPID MANAGEMENT - SUBOPTIMAL CONTROL:
   • LDL 110 mg/dL on Atorvastatin 40mg daily (>40% reduction from baseline 190 mg/dL)
   • GOAL: LDL <70 mg/dL (high-risk patient per 2018 AHA/ACC guidelines)
   • GAP: 40 mg/dL above goal - need intensification
   • Triglycerides 190 mg/dL - elevated, contributing to atherogenic dyslipidemia
   • HDL 38 mg/dL - LOW (protective factor deficiency)

2️⃣ HYPERTENSION - UNCONTROLLED:
   • Current BP 142/88 mmHg on Lisinopril 10mg daily
   • GOAL: <130/80 mmHg (2017 ACC/AHA guidelines for DM + CKD)
   • GAP: 12/8 mmHg above goal - need medication intensification
   • Evidence of target organ damage: LV hypertrophy on echo, microalbuminuria

3️⃣ DIABETES CONTROL - ABOVE GOAL:
   • HbA1c 7.8% on Metformin 1000mg BID
   • GOAL: <7.0% (ADA standard for most adults, individualized)
   • GAP: 0.8% above goal - need medication optimization
   • Complications present: Early diabetic nephropathy (microalbuminuria 45 mcg/mg)

4️⃣ SMOKING HISTORY:
   • Former smoker (quit 2020 - 4 years abstinent) - GOOD progress
   • 30 pack-year history - residual cardiovascular risk
   • Continued abstinence CRITICAL - relapse prevention counseling needed

5️⃣ OBESITY/METABOLIC SYNDROME:
   • BMI 29.5 (overweight, close to obese), waist 42 inches (central obesity)
   • Target: BMI <25, waist <40 inches
   • Weight loss of 15-20 lbs would improve ALL risk factors

6️⃣ COMPOSITE RISK:
   • 10-year ASCVD risk: 22% (HIGH RISK per ACC/AHA calculator)
   • CAC score 250 (moderate plaque burden)
   • Multiple uncontrolled risk factors with target organ damage
   • RECOMMENDATION: Intensive risk factor modification + consider aspirin for primary prevention

recommendations.recommendation: Intensify statin therapy: Increase Atorvastatin 40mg → 80mg daily. Recheck lipid panel in 6 weeks. Goal: LDL <70 mg/dL. Monitor liver enzymes and muscle symptoms.
Add antihypertensive: Start Amlodipine 5mg daily (in addition to Lisinopril 10mg). Monitor BP daily at home. Goal: BP <130/80 mmHg. Follow-up in 4 weeks.
Optimize diabetes management: Add Semaglutide (Ozempic) 0.25mg SQ weekly x 4 weeks, then 0.5mg weekly. Benefits: HbA1c reduction, weight loss, CV protection. Recheck HbA1c in 12 weeks.
Start antiplatelet therapy: Aspirin 81mg daily for primary prevention (10-year ASCVD risk 22%). Avoid NSAIDs for GI protection.
Registered Dietitian referral: Medical nutrition therapy for Mediterranean diet counseling. Target: 1800-2000 kcal/day, <2g sodium, 45g carbs/meal, weight loss 15-20 lbs over 6 months.
Cardiac rehabilitation referral: Phase II program, 12 weeks, 3x/week. Benefits: Supervised exercise, CV risk education, psychosocial support. Insurance covered for high-risk patients.
Home BP monitoring: Measure daily (AM before meds, PM before dinner). Keep log. Target <130/80 mmHg. Report consistently >140/90 or <100/60.
Follow-up labs in 6 weeks: Lipid panel (LDL goal <70), CMP (creatinine, electrolytes), liver enzymes (AST/ALT for statin safety).
Follow-up labs in 12 weeks: HbA1c (goal <7.0%), weight check, assess medication tolerance (GLP-1 GI effects).
Primary care follow-up in 4 weeks: BP control assessment, medication tolerance, home BP log review, weight check.
Cardiology follow-up in 6 months: Comprehensive risk factor reassessment, repeat stress test if symptoms develop, consider advanced lipid therapy if LDL >100 mg/dL despite max therapy.

results.coronaryArteryCalciumScore: 250 (75th percentile for age - moderate plaque burden)
results.smokingStatus: Former smoker (quit 2020 - 4 years abstinent, 30 pack-year history)

input.status: ACTIVE - High-risk cardiovascular patient on comprehensive risk reduction plan. Multiple medication changes initiated (statin intensification, add CCB, add GLP-1 RA, start aspirin). Lifestyle interventions prescribed (diet, exercise, weight loss). Close follow-up scheduled (4 weeks, 6 weeks, 12 weeks). Requires ongoing monitoring and support for adherence.

input.type: Comprehensive Guideline Compliance Review - Chronic Disease Management
input.provider: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD - Internal Medicine & Quality Improvement Committee

guidelines.guidelineName: GOLD 2024 Global Strategy for Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention of COPD
recommendations: Bronchodilator therapy (LABA/LAMA combination)
Inhaled corticosteroid for frequent exacerbations (≥2 per year)
Pulmonary rehabilitation referral
Annual influenza vaccination
Pneumococcal vaccination (PCV20 or PCV15+PPSV23)
Smoking cessation counseling (former smoker - maintain abstinence)
Oxygen therapy assessment if SpO2 <88%
Spirometry annually to track FEV1 decline
gaps: Pulmonary rehabilitation not yet enrolled (patient referred 2 months ago but has not attended - transportation barrier)
Spirometry overdue (last PFTs 14 months ago - guideline recommends annually)
Pneumococcal PCV20 vaccine not administered (patient due, no contraindications)

quantitativeMonitoring.baselineValue: 58% predicted (last measured 14 months ago - GOLD Stage 3 Severe COPD)
quantitativeMonitoring.targetValue: ≥60% predicted (guideline target to maintain stable lung function, avoid decline to GOLD Stage 4)
quantitativeMonitoring.currentStatus: Below target - FEV1 58% is 2% below maintenance goal. Requires repeat PFTs to assess trend (stable vs declining).
quantitativeMonitoring.nextAssessment: Spirometry scheduled 2024-12-10 (2 weeks) - overdue annual assessment

patientReportedOutcomes.guidelineTarget: <10 for low symptom burden (GOLD guideline threshold)
patientReportedOutcomes.interpretation: CAT 22 indicates HIGH symptom impact on daily life (scores 21-30 = high impact, 31-40 = very high impact). Patient reports frequent dyspnea limiting activities, morning cough productive x 30 minutes daily, sleep disturbance 3-4 nights/week.
patientReportedOutcomes.frequency: Every clinic visit (quarterly) per GOLD guidelines + PRN if exacerbation

guidelines.clinicalRationale: Partial compliance due to access barriers (pulmonary rehab transportation issue - working on medical transport approval) and overdue preventive services (PFTs, pneumococcal vaccine - scheduling today). Bronchodilator therapy optimized, exacerbation rate reduced from 3/year → 1/year with current regimen (IMPROVEMENT). Plan: Address gaps at today's visit.

guidelines.guidelineName: ADA 2024 Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes
recommendations: HbA1c monitoring every 3 months until <7.0%, then every 6 months
Metformin as first-line therapy
GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i for patients with ASCVD or high CV risk (patient has CAD)
BP target <130/80 mmHg (patient has HTN)
LDL target <70 mg/dL for ASCVD risk reduction (patient has CAD)
Annual comprehensive foot exam
Annual dilated eye exam (diabetic retinopathy screening)
Annual urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (diabetic nephropathy screening)
Aspirin 81mg daily for secondary prevention (patient has CAD)
Diabetic self-management education (DSME)
Medical nutrition therapy (MNT) with RD
gaps: HbA1c 7.1% above target <7.0% (ADA individualized goal for adults without significant hypoglycemia history)
GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i NOT prescribed despite CAD history (ADA strongly recommends for ASCVD benefit - cardiovascular outcome trials show 14-17% CV event reduction)
Dilated eye exam overdue 8 months (last exam 3/2024 - annual screening recommended)
Diabetic self-management education (DSME) not documented (ADA recommends at diagnosis and annually)

quantitativeMonitoring.targetValue: <7.0% per ADA individualized goal (patient is 65, no severe hypoglycemia history, life expectancy >10 years)
quantitativeMonitoring.currentStatus: Slightly above target - 0.1% gap. Clinically acceptable range (7.0-7.5%) but opportunity for optimization. On Metformin 1000mg BID monotherapy.
quantitativeMonitoring.nextAssessment: Recheck HbA1c 2025-02-15 (3 months) per ADA guideline for above-target values

patientReportedOutcomes.guidelineTarget: <2.0 (minimal distress per ADA psychosocial care guidelines)
patientReportedOutcomes.interpretation: DDS 2.8 indicates MODERATE diabetes distress. Patient reports: (1) Worry about hypoglycemia (scored 4/6 - high), (2) Frustration with blood glucose variability (scored 3/6), (3) Feeling overwhelmed by diabetes management demands (scored 3/6). Emotional burden affecting adherence.
patientReportedOutcomes.frequency: Annually per ADA psychosocial screening recommendations + PRN if adherence concerns

guidelines.clinicalRationale: Partial compliance with opportunities for guideline-directed intensification: (1) HbA1c 7.1% - opportunity to add GLP-1 RA (Semaglutide) for dual benefit: A1c reduction 1.0-1.5% (would achieve <7.0% goal) + proven CV risk reduction per SUSTAIN-6 trial (ADA strongly recommends for patients with CAD). (2) Eye exam scheduling today. (3) DSME referral for diabetes distress support. Clinical rationale for current partial compliance: Recent hospitalization for COPD exacerbation (2 weeks ago) with steroid-induced hyperglycemia - A1c 7.1% likely reflects acute illness, will reassess after recovery and consider GLP-1 RA initiation.

guidelines.guidelineName: 2019 ACC/AHA Guideline on the Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease
recommendations: Statin therapy for LDL reduction (Atorvastatin 40mg daily)
BP control <130/80 mmHg (Lisinopril 20mg + Amlodipine 5mg)
Diabetes management (HbA1c <7.0%)
Aspirin 81mg daily for secondary prevention (patient has CAD - prior MI 2019)
Lifestyle modification (Mediterranean diet, 150 min/week exercise, weight loss if BMI >25)
10-year ASCVD risk calculation (patient has established CAD - automatically high risk)

quantitativeMonitoring.targetValue: <70 mg/dL per ACC/AHA secondary prevention target (patient has CAD)
quantitativeMonitoring.currentStatus: At target - LDL 68 mg/dL meets guideline recommendation. On high-intensity statin (Atorvastatin 40mg daily). Excellent adherence.
quantitativeMonitoring.nextAssessment: Lipid panel 2025-05-01 (6 months) per guideline for patients at target on stable therapy

patientReportedOutcomes.guidelineTarget: ≥75/100 indicates good quality of life with minimal angina symptoms

getOutcomesPrediction.description: Retrieve outcomes prediction records for a patient

input.type: Comprehensive Outcomes Prediction - Multi-Domain Risk Assessment & Recovery Trajectory
input.provider: Dr. Robert Chen, MD - Internal Medicine & Care Transitions Team

modifiableFactors.factor: Medication adherence (current adherence rate estimated 75-80% based on patient interview - reports occasionally forgetting evening doses)
modifiableFactors.impact: Improving adherence from 75% to >95% may reduce COPD exacerbations by 40-50% and decrease 30-day readmission risk from 25-30% to 10-15%. Optimal inhaler technique (currently suboptimal per respiratory therapy assessment) may improve symptom control and reduce rescue inhaler use by 30%.
modifiableFactors.recommendation: CRITICAL INTERVENTION: (1) Simplify regimen - consolidate inhaler to once-daily Tiotropium (instead of BID dosing), use pill organizer for oral meds. (2) Respiratory therapy home visit within 7 days post-discharge to re-educate on proper inhaler technique (spacer use, slow deep inhalation, 10-second breath hold). (3) Set smartphone medication reminders for evening doses. (4) Follow-up call from pharmacy at day 7 to assess adherence barriers. (5) Consider long-acting bronchodilator/ICS combination inhaler if adherence remains suboptimal.

modifiableFactors.factor: Pulmonary rehabilitation attendance (patient has NEVER attended despite 2 prior referrals in 2022 and 2023 - reports transportation barrier and work schedule conflict)
modifiableFactors.impact: Pulmonary rehabilitation participation may reduce COPD exacerbations by 30-40%, decrease dyspnea severity (improve mMRC dyspnea scale from current 3 to 1-2), increase 6-minute walk distance by 25-50 meters, and reduce 30-day readmission risk by 20-25%. Quality of life (SGRQ score) may improve by 10-15 points. Non-participation is a MAJOR modifiable risk factor for poor outcomes.
modifiableFactors.recommendation: ADDRESS BARRIERS IMMEDIATELY: (1) Coordinate with hospital social work to arrange medical transport (Medicaid non-emergency medical transportation approved for pulmonary rehab). (2) Refer to outpatient pulmonary rehab program offering evening sessions (5-7 PM) to accommodate work schedule (patient works part-time 9 AM-2 PM). (3) Set concrete start date within 2-3 weeks post-discharge while patient is motivated. (4) Daughter committed to providing transportation if medical transport unavailable. (5) Physician to emphasize critical importance during 2-week follow-up visit - frame as "essential treatment, not optional."

modifiableFactors.factor: Diabetes optimization (HbA1c 7.1% above goal <7.0%, fasting glucose variability 130-180 mg/dL, diet non-adherence - patient admits to high carbohydrate intake, no regular exercise)
modifiableFactors.impact: Achieving HbA1c <7.0% (from current 7.1%) may reduce microvascular complication risk by 25-30%, improve pneumonia recovery time by 20-30%, and decrease infection susceptibility by 30-40%. Glucose control significantly impacts COPD exacerbation severity and recovery - hyperglycemia (>180 mg/dL) associated with 50% longer hospital stays and increased mortality. Weight loss of 5-10% (current BMI 28, target BMI 25) may improve diabetes control AND reduce COPD dyspnea.
modifiableFactors.recommendation: COMPREHENSIVE DIABETES INTERVENTION: (1) Dietary consultation within 2 weeks - focus on carbohydrate counting (target 45-60g per meal), 2000mg sodium restriction (for heart failure prevention), Mediterranean diet pattern. (2) Initiate structured exercise program: Start with 10 minutes walking daily post-pulmonary rehab graduation, gradually increase to 30 minutes 5 days/week. (3) Increase Metformin to 1500mg BID if tolerated (current 1000mg BID suboptimal dose). (4) Home glucose monitoring twice daily (fasting + 2-hour post-meal) for 2 weeks to identify patterns. (5) Recheck HbA1c in 3 months - if still >7.0%, add GLP-1 RA (semaglutide) for dual glucose/weight benefit. (6) Diabetes education class referral for carbohydrate counting skills.

modifiableFactors.factor: Influenza and pneumococcal vaccination status (patient reports NO flu shot this season 2024-2025, unclear if prior pneumococcal vaccine received - no documentation in chart)
modifiableFactors.impact: Influenza vaccination may reduce COPD exacerbations by 30-40% during flu season (November-March) and decrease pneumonia risk by 50-60% in high-risk patients (COPD, diabetes, age ≥65). Pneumococcal vaccination (PCV20) may reduce invasive pneumococcal disease by 70-80% and pneumonia-related hospitalization by 40-50%. COPD patients have 5-7x higher pneumococcal pneumonia risk - vaccination is CRITICAL protective intervention.
modifiableFactors.recommendation: VACCINATE IMMEDIATELY: (1) Administer Influenza vaccine (high-dose or adjuvanted for age ≥65) before discharge OR within 48 hours post-discharge at PCP office. Patient consent obtained. (2) Administer Pneumococcal PCV20 vaccine today or at 2-week follow-up (if not previously given). Check immunization registry to confirm prior vaccination status. (3) Educate patient: Vaccines are ESSENTIAL for COPD patients, not optional - may prevent future hospitalizations. (4) Document vaccination in chart and patient education materials.

modifiableFactors.factor: Cardiovascular risk factor optimization (LDL cholesterol 95 mg/dL on Atorvastatin 40mg - SUBOPTIMAL for secondary prevention post-MI, target <70 mg/dL per ACC/AHA guidelines; Blood pressure 138/84 mmHg - above goal <130/80 for diabetes/CAD patients)
modifiableFactors.impact: Intensifying statin therapy to achieve LDL <70 mg/dL may reduce recurrent MI risk by 25-30% and cardiovascular mortality by 15-20% (currently 25 mg/dL above target). Optimizing blood pressure to <130/80 mmHg may reduce stroke risk by 30-40%, heart failure progression by 20-25%, and cardiovascular events by 20%. Current suboptimal control increases 5-year cardiovascular event risk from 15% to 25%.
modifiableFactors.recommendation: CARDIOVASCULAR OPTIMIZATION PLAN: (1) INCREASE Atorvastatin 40mg → 80mg daily (high-intensity statin for secondary prevention post-MI per 2018 ACC/AHA guidelines). Recheck lipid panel in 6 weeks - if LDL still >70, consider adding ezetimibe 10mg daily. (2) INCREASE Lisinopril 20mg → 30mg daily to optimize BP control (target <130/80). Monitor potassium and creatinine in 2 weeks (risk of hyperkalemia with ACEI increase). (3) Home blood pressure monitoring twice daily for 2 weeks - patient to bring log to follow-up. (4) Reinforce low-sodium diet (<2000mg/day) for BP control - coordinate with diabetes dietitian. (5) Continue Aspirin 81mg, Metoprolol 50mg (no changes). (6) Cardiology follow-up in 3 months to reassess cardiovascular risk and medication optimization.

recommendations.recommendation: MEDICATION ADHERENCE: Respiratory therapy home visit Day 7 to re-educate inhaler technique (goal >80% correct from current 50%). Provide pill organizer, simplify regimen, pharmacy follow-up calls Day 3 and Day 14. Target >95% adherence.

VACCINATIONS: Administer Influenza vaccine (Fluzone High-Dose) TODAY or within 48 hours at PCP. Administer Pneumococcal PCV20 vaccine TODAY or at 2-week follow-up (check immunization registry for prior vaccine status).
EARLY PCP FOLLOW-UP: Schedule appointment within 7-14 days (NOT 30 days). Assess recovery, medication adherence, early warning signs. CRITICAL to prevent 30-day readmission.

PULMONARY REHABILITATION: Address barriers - arrange Medicaid NEMT for transportation, refer to evening session program (5-7 PM) to accommodate work schedule. START within 2-3 weeks. Emphasize critical importance to patient + family.

DIABETES OPTIMIZATION: (1) Dietary consultation within 2 weeks - carb counting 45-60g/meal, sodium <2000mg/day. (2) INCREASE Metformin 1000mg → 1500mg BID. (3) Home glucose monitoring twice daily x 2 weeks. (4) Recheck HbA1c in 3 months (goal <7.0%).

CARDIOVASCULAR OPTIMIZATION: (1) INCREASE Atorvastatin 40mg → 80mg daily (target LDL <70 mg/dL). (2) INCREASE Lisinopril 20mg → 30mg daily (target BP <130/80). (3) Check K+ and Cr in 2 weeks. (4) Recheck lipid panel in 6 weeks. (5) Cardiology follow-up in 3 months.
EARLY WARNING SIGNS EDUCATION: Call PCP IMMEDIATELY if increased dyspnea, fever, productive cough with color change, chest pain, leg swelling. Early intervention prevents readmission!
FOLLOW-UP SCHEDULE: PCP Week 2, Week 4, Week 8. Dietary consult Week 2. Pulmonary rehab start Week 2-3 (12-week program). HbA1c recheck Month 3. Cardiology Month 3.

results.baselineReadmissionRisk: 25-30% (30-day readmission for COPD exacerbation or pneumonia complications - LACE score 15 HIGH RISK)
results.optimizedReadmissionRisk: 10-15% (50% relative risk reduction with comprehensive intervention - adherence support, vaccination, early follow-up, pulmonary rehab)
results.baselineExacerbationRate: 3 exacerbations per year (2 hospitalizations in past 12 months)
results.optimizedExacerbationRate: 1-2 exacerbations per year (50-60% reduction with medication adherence >95%, pulmonary rehab, vaccination)
results.copdProgression: GOLD Stage 3 Severe (FEV1 58% predicted) - GOAL: stabilize and prevent progression to Stage 4 (<50%) for 5+ years with smoking cessation, adherence, exacerbation prevention
results.diabetesControl: HbA1c 7.1% → TARGET <7.0% in 3 months with Metformin increase, dietary intervention, exercise program
results.cardiovascularRisk: LDL 95 mg/dL → TARGET <70 mg/dL in 6 weeks with statin intensification. BP 138/84 → TARGET <130/80 in 2-4 weeks with ACEI increase.
results.functionalImprovement: 6-minute walk distance 300m → TARGET 325-350m (+25-50m) post-pulmonary rehab. mMRC dyspnea 3 → TARGET 2.
results.modifiableFactorsAddressed: 5 high-yield opportunities: (1) Medication adherence 75% → >95%, (2) Pulmonary rehab NEVER attended → 12-week completion, (3) Diabetes HbA1c 7.1% → <7.0%, (4) Vaccinations missing → flu + pneumococcal administered, (5) Cardiovascular risk factors suboptimal → LDL <70 + BP <130/80 achieved
results.prognosisTransformation: FROM: GUARDED prognosis with 30% readmission risk, frequent exacerbations, progressive decline | TO: GOOD prognosis with 10-15% readmission risk, 50-60% exacerbation reduction, COPD stabilization, cardiovascular risk reduction, improved quality of life
results.5YearSurvival: BASELINE 40-50% (GOLD Stage 3 with frequent exacerbations) → OPTIMIZED 60-70% (with exacerbation prevention, smoking abstinence, optimal medical therapy)

input.status: ACTIVE - Comprehensive Outcomes Optimization in Progress. Tier 1 urgent interventions implemented at discharge (medication adherence tools provided, vaccination administered, early PCP follow-up scheduled 12/2). Tier 2 high-priority interventions scheduled (pulmonary rehab intake 12/9, dietary consult 12/5). Close monitoring for 30-day readmission prevention. Next review at 2-week PCP visit to assess adherence, recovery, early warning signs.

input.findings: Left frontal lobe mass measuring 4.2 x 3.8 x 3.5 cm involving the precentral gyrus with peritumoral edema. Mass effect with 5mm midline shift. Enhancement pattern suggestive of high-grade glioma.
input.assessment: 56-year-old male with left frontal WHO Grade IV glioblastoma involving eloquent motor cortex. Candidate for maximal safe resection with awake craniotomy and intraoperative mapping. High risk for SMA syndrome given tumor location.
input.plan: Proceed with awake craniotomy with intraoperative motor and language mapping. Neurophysiological monitoring throughout. Plan for maximal safe resection followed by adjuvant chemoradiation. Enroll in tumor treating fields protocol postoperatively.

functionalMri.motorMapping: Right hand motor activation 8mm posterior to tumor margin
functionalMri.languageMapping: Broca's area 12mm anterior to lesion, Wernicke's area uninvolved
functionalMri.results: Motor cortex directly adjacent to posterior tumor margin. Language areas preserved with safe corridor identified.
functionalMri.implications: Feasible resection with preserved motor and language function

tractography.corticospinalTract: Displaced medially and posteriorly, intact, 3mm from tumor margin
tractography.findings: Major white matter tracts displaced but preserved. No evidence of tract infiltration.
tractography.surgicalImplications: Subcortical mapping required to preserve CST during deep resection

intraoperativeMonitoring.protocol: Awake craniotomy with sleep-awake-sleep technique. Baseline MEP/SSEP under anesthesia, awake mapping for motor and language, continuous monitoring during resection.
intraoperativeMonitoring.warningCriteria: 50% decrease in MEP amplitude or complete loss triggers immediate surgical pause

tumorCharacteristics.enhancementPattern: Heterogeneous ring enhancement with central necrosis
tumorCharacteristics.massEffect: 5mm leftward midline shift, effacement of left lateral ventricle

ventriculostomy.reason: No obstructive hydrocephalus present. Ventricular system patent.
ventriculostomy.alternativePlan: Mannitol and dexamethasone for perioperative edema management

consultation.neuroOncologyRecommendations: Standard Stupp protocol post-resection: temozolomide with concurrent radiation followed by adjuvant TMZ cycles
consultation.radiationOncologyPlan: 60 Gy in 30 fractions to surgical cavity with 2cm margin, start within 4 weeks post-op
consultation.anesthesiaAssessment: ASA 2, cleared for awake craniotomy procedure. Fiberoptic intubation equipment on standby.

expectedExtentOfResection.limitations: Posterior margin adjacent to motor cortex - preservation of motor function prioritized over extent
expectedExtentOfResection.technique: Fluorescence-guided resection with 5-ALA, ultrasonic aspiration, frameless stereotactic navigation
expectedExtentOfResection.anticipatedResidual: Possible small rim of enhancement at posterior motor cortex interface

smaManagementProtocol.riskAssessment: High risk given proximity to supplementary motor area
smaManagementProtocol.preventionStrategies: Careful dissection along SMA, cortical and subcortical mapping, avoiding retraction injury
smaManagementProtocol.anticipatedSyndrome: Possible transient SMA syndrome with contralateral leg weakness and speech hesitancy
smaManagementProtocol.rehabilitationPlan: Early aggressive PT/OT/ST if syndrome develops. Expected recovery 6-12 weeks.
smaManagementProtocol.patientCounseling: Patient counseled on 20-30% risk of transient SMA syndrome, typically resolves completely

preoperativeMedicalOptimization.medications: Dexamethasone 4mg Q6H started 1/13, Keppra 1000mg BID for seizure prophylaxis, continued home antihypertensives
preoperativeMedicalOptimization.laboratoryWork: CBC, CMP, coagulation panel - all within normal limits as of 1/14
preoperativeMedicalOptimization.imagingCompleted: MRI brain with/without contrast, fMRI, DTI tractography, CTA head if needed intraop
preoperativeMedicalOptimization.dvtProphylaxis: Sequential compression devices intraop, early mobilization post-op, pharmacologic prophylaxis if no hemorrhage

tumorTreatingFields.eligibility: Newly diagnosed GBM, candidate for TTFields per NCCN guidelines
tumorTreatingFields.timing: Initiate after completion of concurrent chemoradiation (approximately 3 months post-op)
tumorTreatingFields.education: Patient educated on device, compliance requirements, scalp care. Educational materials provided.
tumorTreatingFields.enrollment: Will coordinate with Optune device representative for fitting and training post-radiation
tumorTreatingFields.patientAcceptance: Patient agreeable to TTFields therapy, motivated for compliance

recommendations.recommendation: Proceed with awake craniotomy for maximal safe resection within 1 week
Continue dexamethasone 4mg Q6H through surgery, taper post-operatively based on edema and clinical status
Seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam 1000mg BID perioperatively, continue for at least 1 week post-op
Post-operative MRI within 48 hours to assess extent of resection
Neuro-oncology follow-up 2 weeks post-op for treatment planning once pathology available

input.notes: Patient demonstrates good understanding of surgical risks including hemorrhage, infection, stroke, motor/language deficits, and SMA syndrome. Realistic expectations discussed. Family support system strong. Patient motivated for aggressive treatment approach. No contraindications to surgery identified.

getRiskFactors.description: Retrieve risk factors records for a patient

input.factor: Former tobacco use - 45 pack-year history (quit 2 years ago in 2019)
input.findings: Patient has 45 pack-year smoking history (1.5 packs/day for 30 years). Quit smoking 2 years ago (2019) after COPD diagnosis. Used nicotine replacement therapy and varenicline for cessation. Reports no tobacco use since quit date. Lives with non-smoking spouse. Workplace is smoke-free. Occasional exposure to secondhand smoke at family gatherings.
input.assessment: Former heavy smoker with significant cumulative exposure (45 pack-years). Successful sustained abstinence for 2 years. Despite cessation, patient remains at elevated risk for: COPD progression, lung cancer, cardiovascular disease, stroke. Risk decreases over time but never returns to baseline of never-smoker. Current COPD (GOLD Stage III) is direct consequence of smoking history.
input.plan: Continue smoking cessation maintenance. Annual low-dose CT lung cancer screening (patient meets USPSTF criteria: 50-80 years old, 20+ pack-year history, quit <15 years ago). Pulmonary function testing every 6 months to monitor COPD progression. Optimize COPD treatment to prevent further lung damage. Cardiovascular risk assessment with lipid panel, blood pressure monitoring. Reinforce importance of continued abstinence - even one cigarette can trigger relapse.
recommendations: Continue annual low-dose CT lung cancer screening
Pulmonary function tests every 6 months
Cardiovascular risk assessment annually (lipid panel, BP, ECG)
Smoking cessation counseling at each visit to prevent relapse
Influenza and pneumococcal vaccines to prevent respiratory infections
Consider pulmonary rehabilitation to optimize lung function
input.results: Current PFTs: FEV1 58% predicted (GOLD Stage III), FEV1/FVC 0.62 (obstructive pattern). Chest X-ray: Hyperinflation, flattened diaphragms, no nodules. Last lung cancer screening CT (6 months ago): Negative for nodules. Lipid panel: Total cholesterol 220, LDL 145 (borderline high - smoking increases cardiovascular risk). Blood pressure: 138/88 (elevated - smoking-related vascular damage).

getEmergencyInformation.description: Retrieve emergency information records for a patient

input.afterHoursPhone: 555-0911 - Memorial Hospital After-Hours Nurse Line (24/7) or 911 for life-threatening emergencies
warningCriteria: Severe shortness of breath at rest or with minimal activity
Inability to speak in full sentences due to dyspnea
Confusion, altered mental status, or extreme drowsiness
Cyanosis (blue lips or fingernails)
Chest pain not relieved by rest
Oxygen saturation <88% on home oxygen
High fever >102°F with rigors/chills
Coughing up blood (hemoptysis)
Severe leg swelling or pain (possible DVT)
Severe allergic reaction to new medication
whenToCall: Increased shortness of breath not relieved by rescue inhaler
Fever >100.4°F for more than 24 hours
Increased sputum production or change in color (yellow, green, bloody)
Chest pain or pressure
Persistent cough keeping you awake at night
Oxygen saturation dropping below 90% on home oximeter
Side effects from new medications (rash, nausea, dizziness)
Questions about medication dosing or administration
Unable to obtain prescribed medications from pharmacy
Need to reschedule follow-up appointment
input.provider: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD - Attending Pulmonologist
input.findings: Patient discharged after 4-day admission for COPD exacerbation. Patient lives alone but has supportive spouse who visits daily. Patient has home oxygen concentrator (2L NC continuous) and pulse oximeter for monitoring. Patient educated on warning signs requiring immediate medical attention vs. symptoms to call clinic about. Patient verbalized understanding of when to call clinic, when to use after-hours line, and when to call 911.
input.assessment: Patient at moderate-high risk for readmission due to severe COPD (GOLD Stage III), recent exacerbation requiring hospitalization (3rd this year), and living alone. Patient demonstrated good understanding of warning signs and appropriate escalation (call clinic → after-hours line → 911). Emergency contacts (spouse and son) are engaged and available. Patient has working phone, reliable transportation for emergencies (spouse drives), and understands importance of early intervention to prevent deterioration.
recommendations: Post emergency contact information on refrigerator and next to phone
Spouse and son should have copy of current medication list
Keep rescue inhaler and cell phone within reach at all times
Program clinic number, after-hours line, and 911 into speed dial
Wear medical alert bracelet indicating COPD and home oxygen use
Keep list of current medications, allergies, and diagnoses in wallet
Establish plan with emergency contacts for who to call first in emergency
Consider medical alert system (LifeAlert) for fall/emergency situations given patient lives alone
input.results: Patient Education Assessment: Patient able to list 3 warning signs requiring 911 call (severe SOB, chest pain, confusion). Patient correctly demonstrated use of pulse oximeter and stated target SpO2 >90%. Patient correctly identified when to call clinic vs after-hours line vs 911. Patient has written discharge instructions and emergency contact card.

primaryDiagnosis.diagnosis: COPD exacerbation (GOLD Stage III) with community-acquired pneumonia (right lower lobe)

treatmentTimeline.response: Good response - WBC decreased from 14.2 to 9.8, fever resolved day 2, CXR improved

treatmentTimeline.duration: 5 days total (discharge on day 4 with 1-day supply)
treatmentTimeline.response: Dyspnea improved, FEV1 increased from 45% to 58% predicted

treatmentTimeline.response: Wheezing resolved by day 2, patient able to use MDI with spacer

input.diseaseStatus: Stable - COPD exacerbation resolving, pneumonia improving. Patient returned to baseline respiratory function (FEV1 58% predicted, consistent with GOLD Stage III COPD). No acute distress. Baseline functional status restored.
input.provider: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD - Attending Pulmonologist
input.plan: Discharge home with: (1) Complete 5-day prednisone course (1 day remaining), (2) Start azithromycin 250mg daily x4 days for atypical coverage, (3) Resume home COPD medications: fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 BID, tiotropium 18mcg daily, albuterol PRN, (4) Continue home oxygen 2L continuous, (5) Pulmonology follow-up in 2 weeks with Dr. Rodriguez, (6) Obtain outpatient PFTs 2 days before pulmonology visit, (7) Smoking cessation reinforcement, (8) Pneumococcal and influenza vaccines if not up to date, (9) Consider pulmonary rehab referral at follow-up. Patient to monitor for worsening dyspnea, fever, sputum changes - call clinic if symptoms worsen.
recommendations: Complete full 5-day prednisone course (1 day remaining after discharge)
Complete 4-day azithromycin course for atypical pathogen coverage
Resume all home COPD maintenance medications as prescribed
Continue home oxygen 2L continuous (baseline requirement)
Pulmonology follow-up with Dr. Rodriguez in 2 weeks
Outpatient PFTs 2 days before pulmonology appointment
Smoking cessation counseling at follow-up (patient quit 2019, reinforce sustained abstinence)
Ensure pneumococcal and influenza vaccines up to date
Pulmonary rehabilitation referral to be considered at follow-up
Patient education reinforced: inhaler technique, oxygen safety, when to seek care

Sputum culture: Normal respiratory flora. Blood cultures: No growth.

getPrognosis.description: Retrieve prognosis records for a patient

input.shortTerm: Expected to return to baseline functional status within 2-3 weeks with adherence to treatment plan. COPD exacerbation should resolve with completion of antibiotic course and gradual steroid taper. Close monitoring for recurrent dyspnea or worsening symptoms needed.
input.longTerm: COPD GOLD Stage III - progressive disease with declining lung function expected. Estimated FEV1 decline of 30-50 mL/year. High risk of future exacerbations (2-3 per year likely given smoking history and comorbidities). 5-year mortality approximately 40-50% without lung transplant. Quality of life will depend on smoking cessation maintenance, medication adherence, and pulmonary rehabilitation participation. Risk of developing cor pulmonale and respiratory failure over 5-10 years.
riskFactors: COPD GOLD Stage III (FEV1 58% predicted) - major risk for decline
Former heavy smoker - 45 pack-year history increases exacerbation risk
Age 68 - advanced age associated with poorer outcomes
Multiple comorbidities: CAD, diabetes, hypertension - increase mortality risk
History of 3 COPD exacerbations in past year - predictor of future exacerbations
Low socioeconomic status - barrier to medication adherence
Social isolation - lack of support system for disease management
protectiveFactors: Quit smoking 2 years ago (2019) - slows disease progression
Strong medication adherence historically - good compliance with inhalers
Completed pulmonary rehabilitation in 2021 - improves outcomes
Close follow-up with pulmonology - regular PFT monitoring
Good insight into disease - recognizes warning signs early
Supportive spouse - assists with medication management
Access to specialized pulmonary care at Memorial Hospital
input.motivationFactors: Patient highly motivated to maintain independence and avoid rehospitalization. Grandson's upcoming wedding in 6 months serves as strong motivator for adherence. Fear of oxygen dependence drives compliance. Positive response to previous interventions reinforces belief in treatment efficacy.
input.previousTreatmentResponse: Excellent response to previous COPD exacerbation treatments - consistently shows improvement with antibiotic/steroid therapy. Responded well to pulmonary rehabilitation in 2021 with improved 6-minute walk distance (320m to 410m). Good response to LABA/LAMA therapy with FEV1 improvement from 52% to 58% over 6 months. No history of treatment failures or non-response.
input.insightLevel: Good insight into COPD disease process. Recognizes early warning signs of exacerbation (increased dyspnea, sputum color change). Understands importance of medication adherence and smoking cessation. Able to articulate treatment plan and follow-up requirements. Asks appropriate questions about disease management. Demonstrates understanding of COPD action plan.
input.mortality: 5-year mortality risk approximately 40-50% based on COPD GOLD Stage III, age, comorbidities (CAD, diabetes), and exacerbation history. BODE index score 5 (FEV1 58%, 6MWT 410m, mMRC dyspnea 2, BMI 28) predicts 4-year survival ~60%. Risk of respiratory failure requiring intubation during future exacerbations: 15-20%. Sudden cardiac death risk elevated due to CAD and COPD interaction.
input.functionalStatus: Currently independent in all ADLs (bathing, dressing, toileting, eating, transferring). Requires assistance with some IADLs (heavy housework, grocery shopping due to dyspnea). Baseline ambulatory with cane for long distances. Oxygen-independent at rest and with usual activities. 6-minute walk test baseline: 410 meters (68% predicted). mMRC dyspnea scale: Grade 2 (stops for breath when walking at own pace on level ground). Expected to return to this baseline within 2-3 weeks post-discharge.
input.provider: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD - Attending Hospitalist
input.findings: Patient responded well to treatment for COPD exacerbation with resolution of acute symptoms. Chest X-ray improved from admission (RLL infiltrate resolved). Labs normalized (WBC 14.2 → 9.8). Oxygen saturation stable on room air (94-96%). Pulmonary exam shows decreased wheezing compared to admission. Ambulating without distress. Vital signs stable.
input.assessment: 68-year-old with COPD GOLD Stage III admitted for acute exacerbation with community-acquired pneumonia, now clinically improved and ready for discharge. Risk stratification: High risk for future exacerbations based on smoking history, disease severity, and recent exacerbation frequency (3 in past year). Requires close outpatient follow-up and aggressive preventive measures. Prognosis guarded due to progressive nature of COPD and significant comorbidity burden.
input.plan: Discharge home with completion of antibiotic course (5 more days levofloxacin), prednisone taper over 10 days, continuation of LABA/LAMA therapy, and pulmonary rehabilitation referral. Close follow-up with pulmonology in 2 weeks with repeat PFTs. Smoking cessation counseling and support. Advance care planning discussion at next visit. COPD action plan provided with instructions for early recognition and treatment of exacerbations.
recommendations: Complete 14-day antibiotic course (levofloxacin 750mg daily - 5 days remaining)
Complete prednisone taper: 40mg x3d, 30mg x3d, 20mg x2d, 10mg x2d
Continue LABA/LAMA inhaler (Anoro Ellipta) daily - DO NOT discontinue
Continue rescue albuterol as needed for dyspnea
Pulmonology follow-up in 2 weeks with Dr. Rodriguez - repeat PFTs scheduled
Referral to pulmonary rehabilitation program - start within 4 weeks
Annual influenza vaccination (due in 2 months)
Pneumococcal vaccination update (PPSV23 needed - not up to date)
Advance care planning discussion at next visit - consider goals of care
COPD action plan education - recognize and treat exacerbations early
Smoking cessation maintenance - continue support group attendance
Optimize nutrition - dietitian referral for weight management (BMI 28)
input.results: CXR (11/28): RLL infiltrate resolved, hyperinflation consistent with COPD, no acute process. WBC 9.8 (normalized from 14.2 on admission). SpO2 94-96% on room air at rest and with ambulation. 6-minute walk test: 380m (down from baseline 410m, expected to improve). PFTs from 3 months ago: FEV1 58% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.52 (GOLD Stage III). BNP 180 (stable, no heart failure).
input.notes: Patient demonstrates excellent understanding of disease process and treatment plan. Wife present for discharge education and will assist with medication management. Home environment assessed - stairs manageable with rest breaks. Financial assistance provided for medication copays through hospital charity program. Patient verbalized understanding of warning signs requiring medical attention. Emergency contact information updated. DME company contacted for nebulizer supplies.

getCardiologyAssessment.description: Retrieve cardiology assessment records for a patient

echocardiogram.wallMotion: Normal global LV systolic function. No regional wall motion abnormalities (RWMA). All segments contract normally.
echocardiogram.valvularFunction: Mild mitral regurgitation (Grade 1/4). Trace tricuspid regurgitation. Aortic and pulmonic valves normal. No stenosis. RVSP 32 mmHg (normal).

input.electrocardiogram: Sinus rhythm at 78 bpm. Normal axis. PR interval 168 ms. QRS duration 92 ms. QTc 420 ms. No ST-segment elevation or depression. T-wave inversions in leads V1-V2 (normal variant vs old anterior ischemia). No acute ischemic changes. Old Q waves in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) consistent with prior inferior MI.
input.cardiacCatheterization: Left heart catheterization performed 11/26/2024. Coronary angiography: Left main patent. LAD: 40% mid-vessel stenosis (non-obstructive). LCx: patent. RCA: 100% proximal occlusion with collaterals from LAD (chronic total occlusion from prior MI 2019). LV gram: EF 55%, inferior hypokinesis. Hemodynamics: LVEDP 14 mmHg. No intervention performed. Medical management recommended.
input.stressTest: Pharmacologic stress test with regadenoson 11/20/2024. Exercise contraindicated due to COPD. Nuclear imaging: No reversible perfusion defects. Fixed inferior defect consistent with prior MI. Achieved adequate heart rate response (85% max predicted). No chest pain. No significant ST changes. Duke Treadmill Score not applicable (pharmacologic). Negative for inducible ischemia.
scheduledProcedures: Repeat echocardiogram in 6 months (05/2025) - monitor mitral regurgitation progression
Coronary CT angiography in 1 year (11/2025) - assess LAD stenosis progression
Holter monitor in 3 months (02/2025) - evaluate palpitations, rule out arrhythmias
Cardiac MRI with delayed enhancement - assess viability of inferior wall (RCA territory) - scheduled 01/15/2025
additionalTestingOrdered: Lipid panel fasting - recheck in 3 months on atorvastatin 80mg (target LDL <70)
BNP level - baseline for future comparison if heart failure symptoms develop
Carotid ultrasound - screen for carotid stenosis (vascular risk assessment)
Ankle-brachial index (ABI) - assess for peripheral arterial disease
HbA1c - monitor diabetes control (impacts CAD progression)

coronaryArteryDiseaseRiskFactors.diabetes: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, well-controlled on metformin. HbA1c 6.8% (target <7%). Diagnosed 2015.
coronaryArteryDiseaseRiskFactors.hypertension: Hypertension, well-controlled on lisinopril 20mg and amlodipine 5mg. Home BP avg 128/78 mmHg. Diagnosed 2012.
coronaryArteryDiseaseRiskFactors.smoking: Former tobacco use - quit 2 years ago (2019) after MI. 45 pack-year history (1.5 PPD x 30 years). Enrolled in smoking cessation program.
coronaryArteryDiseaseRiskFactors.familyHistory: Strong family history of CAD. Father: MI at age 52, died age 65 (CAD). Mother: CABG at age 68. Brother: stents at age 55.

input.provider: Dr. James Martinez, MD, FACC - Cardiology
recommendations: Continue aspirin 81mg daily + clopidogrel 75mg daily (dual antiplatelet therapy - minimum 1 year post-MI, likely lifelong given RCA occlusion)
Continue atorvastatin 80mg nightly - recheck lipid panel in 3 months (target LDL <70, achieved at 65 last check)
Continue lisinopril 20mg daily + amlodipine 5mg daily - target BP <130/80
Continue metoprolol succinate 50mg daily - heart rate goal 60-70 bpm at rest
Maintain metformin for diabetes - coordinate with endocrinology, target HbA1c <7%
Smoking cessation maintenance - continue support group, nicotine replacement PRN if cravings return
Cardiac rehabilitation program enrollment - 12-week supervised exercise program (coordinate with pulmonary rehab)
Mediterranean diet counseling - dietitian referral for cardiac nutrition education
Daily weights monitoring - call if weight gain >3 lbs in 2 days (early heart failure warning)
Activity: gradual increase to 30 min moderate exercise 5x/week (monitor symptoms, use pulse oximeter)
Follow-up with cardiology in 3 months (Dr. Martinez) - review Holter results, assess symptoms
Annual flu vaccination (due in 2 months) - CRITICAL for cardiac + COPD patients
Avoid NSAIDs (increase MI risk + GI bleeding on dual antiplatelet) - use acetaminophen for pain
Cardiac warning signs education: Call 911 for chest pain, severe dyspnea, palpitations >20 min, syncope
input.results: Echo (11/28/2024): EF 55%, LV normal size, inferior wall hypokinesis, mild MR. Cath (11/26/2024): RCA 100% occluded (chronic), LAD 40% stenosis, LCx patent, LVEDP 14 mmHg. Stress test (11/20/2024): Negative for ischemia, fixed inferior defect. ECG (11/29/2024): Sinus rhythm, old inferior Q waves, no acute changes. Lipids (11/15/2024): TC 165, LDL 65, HDL 42, TG 180. HbA1c (11/15/2024): 6.8%. BNP (11/25/2024): 145 pg/mL (normal <100, mild elevation likely from COPD exacerbation stress).
input.status: Active Cardiology Follow-up - Stable CAD, Close Monitoring Required

Fixed inferior defect consistent with prior MI. Achieved adequate heart rate response (85% max predicted). No chest pain. No significant ST changes. Duke Treadmill Score not applicable (pharmacologic). Negative for inducible ischemia.

getLifestyleCounseling.description: Retrieve lifestyle counseling records for a patient

input.type: Outpatient Pulmonology Visit - COPD Lifestyle Counseling
input.provider: Dr. Sarah Chen, Pulmonologist & Jane Smith, RD
input.findings: Patient is 67-year-old former smoker (quit 6 months ago) with COPD GOLD Stage III. BMI 32 (obese), sedentary lifestyle, reports poor sleep quality due to nocturnal dyspnea. High sodium diet contributing to lower extremity edema. Experiencing anxiety related to disease progression.
input.assessment: Lifestyle factors significantly impacting COPD management: Obesity limiting exercise tolerance and contributing to sleep apnea. Dietary sodium excess causing fluid retention. Sedentary lifestyle accelerating deconditioning. Sleep disturbance from nocturnal symptoms. Smoking cessation maintained but requires ongoing support.
input.plan: Comprehensive lifestyle modification program: 1) Cardiac rehab referral for supervised exercise 2) Nutrition consult for DASH diet + weight loss 3) Sleep study for suspected OSA 4) Stress management referral 5) Smoking cessation program enrollment for relapse prevention 6) Alcohol screening and brief intervention

recommendations.recommendation: Follow DASH diet with 2g sodium restriction, increase fruits/vegetables, reduce processed foods
Enroll in pulmonary rehabilitation 3x/week for 12 weeks, daily walking 10-15 minutes with gradual progression
Target weight loss 1-2 lbs/week, goal BMI <30, nutritionist follow-up monthly
Sleep study ordered for suspected OSA, elevate head of bed 30 degrees, avoid eating 3 hours before bedtime
Continue abstinence, enroll in relapse prevention program, nicotine replacement PRN for cravings

input.results: Patient verbalized understanding of lifestyle modifications. Expressed motivation for pulmonary rehab. Concerned about dietary changes but willing to work with nutritionist. Sleep study scheduled for 12/5/24. Smoking cessation program enrollment completed.
input.notes: Comprehensive lifestyle counseling session lasting 45 minutes. Discussed impact of obesity on respiratory function and exercise capacity. Reviewed sodium restriction benefits for reducing lower extremity edema. Patient motivated by recent grandchild's birth - wants to improve functional status. Provided written materials on DASH diet, pulmonary rehabilitation benefits, and smoking relapse prevention. Will reassess lifestyle modifications at 3-month follow-up.
input.status: Active Lifestyle Modification Program - Multiple Interventions
input.diet: DASH diet recommended: 2g sodium restriction, increase fruits (4-5 servings/day) and vegetables (4-5 servings/day), whole grains, lean protein (fish 2x/week), limit red meat, reduce processed foods and saturated fats. Dietitian consult scheduled for 12/1/24. Goal: Improve nutritional status while supporting weight loss.
input.exercise: Pulmonary rehabilitation enrollment: 3 sessions/week for 12 weeks starting 12/10/24. Home exercise prescription: Daily walking 10-15 minutes at comfortable pace (able to talk while walking), gradually increase to 30 minutes over 8 weeks. Upper extremity exercises with 2-lb weights 3x/week. Avoid strenuous exertion. Use rescue inhaler 15 minutes before exercise if needed.
input.weight: Current: 210 lbs, Height: 5'8", BMI: 32 (obese). Target: Gradual weight loss of 1-2 lbs/week, goal weight 180 lbs (BMI 27.4) over 6 months. Weight loss expected to improve dyspnea, exercise tolerance, and sleep quality. Weekly weight monitoring, nutritionist follow-up monthly to adjust plan.
input.stress: Patient reports moderate anxiety related to COPD progression and fear of exacerbations. Stress management interventions: Referral to behavioral health for cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT). Recommend mindfulness meditation app (Calm or Headspace) 10 minutes daily. Deep breathing exercises (pursed-lip breathing, diaphragmatic breathing) to reduce dyspnea-related anxiety. Pulmonary support group referral.
input.sleep: Reports poor sleep quality: Waking 3-4 times nightly with dyspnea, daytime fatigue, loud snoring per spouse (suggests OSA). Sleep hygiene counseling provided: Elevate head of bed 30 degrees, maintain consistent sleep schedule, avoid caffeine after 2pm, limit screen time 1 hour before bed. Sleep study ordered for 12/5/24 to evaluate for obstructive sleep apnea. If OSA confirmed, CPAP therapy will improve oxygenation and sleep quality.
input.alcohol: Current use: 2-3 beers on weekends (moderate use). Alcohol screening (AUDIT-C score: 3 - low risk). Brief intervention provided: Discussed interaction between alcohol and medications (sedation with benzodiazepines if prescribed for anxiety). Alcohol can worsen GERD and nocturnal dyspnea. Recommend limiting to 1 drink/day or less, avoid alcohol within 3 hours of bedtime. Patient agreeable to reduction.

input.type: Annual Wellness Visit - Comprehensive Preventive Care Review
input.provider: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD - Primary Care & Jane Smith, NP
input.findings: Patient is 55-year-old with family history of colon cancer (father diagnosed at age 62), 20 pack-year smoking history (quit 2 years ago), BMI 28 (overweight). Preventive care screening review identifies multiple areas requiring attention: colonoscopy never performed (DUE - patient is 55, family history warrants screening at age 50), last mammogram 18 months ago (OVERDUE for annual screening), cervical cancer screening up-to-date (Pap/HPV co-test 6 months ago, negative), no lung cancer screening despite 20 pack-year smoking history and age 55 (USPSTF criteria met), no prostate screening discussed (female patient - N/A), no AAA screening performed (female, low risk), depression screening (PHQ-9) completed today (score 3 - minimal symptoms), alcohol screening (AUDIT-C) completed today (score 2 - low risk), immunization record reviewed showing gaps.
input.assessment: Multiple preventive care gaps identified requiring immediate action: 1) Colonoscopy OVERDUE (age 55 + family history should have started at 50) - HIGH PRIORITY, 2) Mammography overdue for annual screening - schedule within 1 month, 3) Lung cancer screening INDICATED (20 pack-year history, age 55, quit <15 years ago) - meets USPSTF criteria for low-dose CT, 4) Depression screening negative (PHQ-9: 3), 5) Alcohol screening low risk (AUDIT-C: 2), 6) Immunizations need updating: Tdap booster due, annual flu vaccine due, pneumococcal vaccine (PPSV23) due at age 65, shingles vaccine (Shingrix) recommended starting age 50.
input.plan: Comprehensive preventive care plan implemented today: 1) Colonoscopy referral to GI - URGENT given family history and age, counseled on bowel prep and procedure, 2) Mammography scheduled for 12/5/2024 at Radiology Associates, 3) Lung cancer screening referral - low-dose chest CT ordered, shared decision-making discussion documented, 4) Continue depression monitoring, no intervention needed at this time, 5) Continue current alcohol use pattern (social only), 6) Immunization update plan created - administer today: Tdap booster, Influenza vaccine (2024-2025), schedule Shingrix series (2 doses), 7) Patient education provided on cancer screening importance, smoking cessation maintenance, healthy diet and exercise.

recommendations.recommendation: Schedule colonoscopy URGENTLY - overdue for age 55 with family history of colon cancer. Should have started at age 50. GI referral placed.
Annual mammogram OVERDUE - last screening 18 months ago. Scheduled for 12/5/2024 at Radiology Associates.
Low-dose chest CT recommended - patient meets USPSTF criteria (age 55, 20 pack-year smoking history, quit 2 years ago). Shared decision-making completed.
Update immunizations: Tdap booster administered today, Influenza vaccine administered today, Shingrix series scheduled (dose 1 today, dose 2 in 2-6 months).

input.results: Patient verbalized understanding of preventive care screening gaps and agreed to all recommended interventions. Expressed concern about colonoscopy (never had one before) - provided detailed education on procedure, bowel prep, sedation, and importance given family history. Received Tdap and Influenza vaccines today without adverse reactions. Understands lung cancer screening is annual low-dose CT while she remains high-risk. Scheduled follow-up appointments: Mammography 12/5/2024, Colonoscopy (pending GI scheduling - target within 3 months), Lung CT (pending insurance authorization).
input.status: Active Preventive Care Plan - Multiple Screenings Scheduled
input.colonoscopyDueAge: AGE 50 (family history of colon cancer - father diagnosed at 62). Patient is now 55 years old - OVERDUE by 5 years. URGENT colonoscopy referral placed to GI. Patient counseled on importance given family history increases risk by 2-3 fold. Bowel prep instructions provided. Target: Complete within 3 months.
input.mammographyStatus: OVERDUE - Last mammogram: 4/15/2023 (18 months ago). Annual screening recommended for women age 40-74. Patient is 55 years old with no personal/family history of breast cancer. Scheduled: 12/5/2024 at Radiology Associates (digital mammography with tomosynthesis). Patient educated on self-breast exam and to report any lumps, nipple discharge, or skin changes immediately. Next annual mammogram: 12/2025.
input.cervicalScreeningStatus: UP-TO-DATE - Last Pap/HPV co-test: 5/10/2024 (6 months ago) - NEGATIVE for both. Age 55, no history of abnormal results. Next screening: 5/2027 (3-year interval for co-testing per USPSTF). Patient counseled that cervical cancer screening continues until age 65 if adequate negative screening history. No HPV vaccination (age >45, not recommended).
input.prostateCancerScreening: N/A - Female patient. (If male: PSA screening discussion recommended starting age 50-55 based on individual risk factors and shared decision-making per USPSTF.)
input.aaaScreening: NOT INDICATED - Female patient, low risk. (AAA screening with ultrasound recommended for MEN age 65-75 who have ever smoked per USPSTF. Women generally not screened unless strong family history.)
input.alcoholScreening: COMPLETED TODAY - AUDIT-C administered: Score 2/12 (LOW RISK). Drinking pattern: 1-2 glasses of wine on weekends only (social occasions), typically 2-4 drinks per month. No binge drinking (≥4 drinks per occasion for women). No history of alcohol use disorder, DUIs, or alcohol-related health problems. Patient educated on low-risk drinking limits (≤7 drinks/week for women, ≤3 drinks/occasion). No intervention needed at this time. Continue annual alcohol screening per USPSTF.
