getTreatmentPlans.description: Retrieve treatment plans records for a patient

immediateInterventions.activity: Gradual activity advancement protocol: Bed rest → chair sitting (start day 1) → ambulate in room with standby assistance (day 2-3) → ambulate in hallway 50 feet TID with walker (day 4-5) → independent ambulation with walker (day 6-7). Avoid strenuous exertion for 2 weeks post-discharge. Pulmonary rehabilitation enrollment (3x/week for 12 weeks) starting 1 week after discharge. Deep breathing exercises Q2H while awake. Incentive spirometry to 1500 mL Q1H x 10 reps. Energy conservation techniques (rest between activities, plan high-energy tasks for morning). Oxygen via nasal cannula at 2L continuous (maintain SpO2 >92%). Elevate head of bed 30-45 degrees to optimize breathing.
immediateInterventions.diet: Diabetic, heart-healthy diet (2g sodium restriction, <2000 calories/day, carbohydrate consistent meals). Small frequent meals (6 per day) - large meals increase work of breathing. Adequate protein for healing (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day, ~90g protein daily). Encourage PO fluid intake 1.5-2L/day unless contraindicated (maintain hydration for secretion clearance). Avoid gas-producing foods (carbonated beverages, beans, cabbage) - increase abdominal pressure and dyspnea. Nutritionist consult: meal planning education, diabetic food choices, cooking modifications for low sodium. Nutrition supplements: Boost Glucose Control TID if PO intake inadequate.
immediateInterventions.medications:
DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS - Continue as prescribed:

1. COPD/RESPIRATORY:
   • Albuterol (ProAir HFA) 90mcg inhaler - 2 puffs Q4-6H PRN for dyspnea/wheezing (rescue inhaler)
   • Ipratropium/Albuterol (Combivent) - 1 puff QID (maintenance bronchodilator)
   • Fluticasone/Salmeterol (Advair Diskus) 250/50 - 1 puff BID (ICS/LABA for COPD maintenance)
   • Prednisone 40mg PO daily x 3 days, then 20mg daily x 4 days, then STOP (steroid taper for COPD exacerbation)

2. ANTIBIOTICS:
   • Levofloxacin 750mg PO daily x 5 more days (started in hospital, total 7-day course for community-acquired pneumonia)

3. DIABETES:
   • Metformin 1000mg PO BID with meals
   • Insulin glargine (Lantus) 20 units subcutaneous QHS

4. CARDIOVASCULAR:
   • Lisinopril 10mg PO daily (ACE inhibitor for HTN + diabetes)
   • Amlodipine 5mg PO daily (calcium channel blocker for HTN)
   • Atorvastatin 40mg PO QHS (statin for hyperlipidemia + diabetes)

5. PROPHYLAXIS:
   • Aspirin 81mg PO daily (cardiovascular risk reduction - secondary prevention)
   • Omeprazole 20mg PO daily (PPI for GERD + aspirin GI protection)

6. SUPPORTIVE:
   • Docusate sodium 100mg PO BID (stool softener - prevent straining with dyspnea)
   • Acetaminophen 650mg PO Q6H PRN for pain/fever (avoid NSAIDs with lisinopril + aspirin)

MEDICATION EDUCATION:
• Inhaler technique teaching completed (MDI with spacer, rinse mouth after ICS)
• Prednisone taper schedule written - DO NOT stop abruptly, complete full taper
• Levofloxacin: Take 2 hours before or after antacids, complete full course even if feeling better
• Blood glucose monitoring: Check fasting + before dinner while on prednisone (steroids raise blood sugar)
• Med reconciliation completed with pharmacy - no duplicates, no drug interactions identified
immediateInterventions.monitoring: Daily weight (report gain >2 lbs/day or >5 lbs/week - fluid overload), daily blood glucose (fasting + pre-dinner while on prednisone, report >250 mg/dL), oxygen saturation monitoring (maintain SpO2 >92% on 2L O2, report <90%), temperature (report fever >100.4°F - infection), respiratory rate/dyspnea assessment (report increased shortness of breath, use of accessory muscles, unable to speak full sentences), sputum color/amount (report yellow-green purulent sputum - infection), pedal edema (report worsening lower extremity swelling)
immediateInterventions.equipment: Home oxygen concentrator (2L continuous via nasal cannula, portable O2 tank for outings), pulse oximeter (check SpO2 BID and with activity), walker (standard walker for ambulation safety), raised toilet seat (reduce energy expenditure), shower chair (conserve energy during bathing), bedside commode (nighttime use to reduce fall risk), nebulizer machine (for ipratropium/albuterol treatments if MDI technique difficult), diabetic glucometer + testing strips (BG monitoring)
immediateInterventions.patientEducation: COPD exacerbation warning signs: Increased dyspnea, increased sputum production, change in sputum color (yellow-green), fever, chest tightness, increased cough, decreased exercise tolerance, confusion (hypoxia). Pneumonia prevention: Hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts, annual flu vaccine, pneumococcal vaccine (age 65+), good oral hygiene. Smoking cessation: Remain abstinent (quit 2 years ago), avoid triggers, nicotine replacement if cravings, support group enrollment. Diabetes management: Medication adherence, blood glucose monitoring, foot care, annual eye exams. When to call doctor: Fever >100.4°F, worsening dyspnea not relieved by rescue inhaler, chest pain, SpO2 <90%, unable to eat/drink, confusion, swelling in legs. When to call 911: Severe difficulty breathing, chest pain with radiation, altered mental status, lips/fingernails turning blue.

pendingProcedures: Pulmonary function testing (PFTs) - outpatient in 6 weeks after acute exacerbation resolves (assess COPD severity, FEV1, establish baseline)
Chest CT scan - outpatient in 3 months if symptoms not fully resolved (r/o underlying structural lung disease, bronchiectasis, malignancy given smoking history)
Echocardiogram - outpatient in 2 months (assess for pulmonary hypertension from COPD, evaluate LV function given diabetes + HTN)
Colonoscopy - OVERDUE (age 68, last at 58 - 10 years ago, due for repeat). Schedule when acute illness resolved (2-3 months).
Diabetic eye exam - outpatient ophthalmology (dilated retinal exam for diabetic retinopathy screening, last exam 18 months ago - overdue)
rehabilitationReferrals: Pulmonary rehabilitation - PRIORITY referral. 3 sessions/week x 12 weeks. Benefits: Improve exercise tolerance, reduce dyspnea, education on COPD management, breathing techniques (pursed-lip breathing, diaphragmatic breathing), energy conservation, anxiety management. Start 1 week post-discharge. Location: Memorial Hospital Pulmonary Rehab Center. Insurance pre-authorization obtained.

Physical therapy - Home health PT 2x/week x 4 weeks. Goals: Improve functional mobility, gait training with walker, stair negotiation, fall risk assessment, strengthening exercises (lower extremity focus), endurance training. Teach safe transfer techniques. Progress to outpatient PT if further therapy needed.

Occupational therapy - Home health OT 1x/week x 4 weeks. Goals: ADL training with energy conservation techniques (bathing, dressing, meal prep), home safety assessment and modifications, adaptive equipment training (shower chair, raised toilet seat, reacher), breathing techniques integrated into ADLs.

Cardiac rehabilitation - Referral placed (diabetes + HTN + hyperlipidemia = high CV risk). 3 sessions/week x 12 weeks after medical clearance. ECG monitoring during exercise, supervised aerobic training, resistance training, nutrition counseling, stress management. Can coordinate with pulmonary rehab (some programs offer combined pulmonary-cardiac rehab).

Diabetes education - Outpatient diabetes self-management education (DSME) with certified diabetes educator (CDE). Topics: Blood glucose monitoring, medication administration (insulin technique), hypoglycemia recognition/treatment, sick day management, foot care, nutrition, complications prevention. 4 sessions (initial + 3 follow-up). Insurance covers DSME for Medicare.
longTermGoals: Prevent future COPD exacerbations requiring hospitalization (goal: 0 exacerbations in next year) - Strategies: Medication adherence (daily ICS/LABA), avoid respiratory infections (hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts, vaccines), early intervention for URI symptoms (have prednisone + antibiotic pack at home for provider-approved self-start), smoking abstinence maintenance, pulmonary rehab completion.

Optimize diabetes control and prevent complications (HbA1c goal: <7.5% for older adult with comorbidities, current 7.1% - maintain) - Strategies: Medication adherence (metformin + insulin), blood glucose monitoring (adjust insulin dose PRN), diabetic diet compliance, weight loss (target BMI <30, current 32), annual diabetic complication screening (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy, foot exams), cardiovascular risk reduction (BP control, statin therapy, aspirin).

Improve functional status and quality of life (return to baseline independence in ADLs, reduce dyspnea with daily activities) - Strategies: Pulmonary rehabilitation (increase exercise tolerance), energy conservation techniques, oxygen therapy as prescribed (maintain SpO2 >92%), physical/occupational therapy (strengthen, improve mobility), treat depression if develops (chronic illness burden).

Cardiovascular risk reduction (prevent MI, stroke) - Strategies: BP goal <130/80 (on lisinopril + amlodipine, home BP monitoring), LDL goal <100 mg/dL (on atorvastatin 40mg, annual lipid panel), aspirin 81mg daily (secondary prevention), smoking abstinence, diabetes control, weight loss, cardiac rehabilitation enrollment.

Maintain smoking abstinence (quit 2 years ago, 45 pack-year history) - Strategies: Avoid triggers (alcohol, stress, social situations with smokers), nicotine replacement therapy if cravings (patch, gum, lozenge), behavioral counseling (support group, quitline 1-800-QUIT-NOW), stress management techniques, celebrate milestones (2-year anniversary!), educate on COPD progression if relapse (FEV1 decline accelerates).

Fall prevention (high risk: age 68, deconditioning, oxygen tubing, medications causing orthostatic hypotension) - Strategies: Home safety assessment (OT evaluation, remove tripping hazards, install grab bars, adequate lighting), assistive device use (walker until strength/balance improve), fall risk medications review (lisinopril, amlodipine can cause dizziness), vitamin D supplementation if deficient (bone health), lower extremity strengthening (PT exercises), vision screening (annual eye exam).
shortTermGoals: Resolution of pneumonia (complete antibiotic course, clinical improvement, afebrile x 48 hours, improved oxygenation) - Timeframe: 7-10 days. Monitoring: Temperature, SpO2, respiratory symptoms (cough, sputum, dyspnea), appetite/energy. Follow-up: PCP visit in 1 week (clinical exam, assess response to antibiotics). Consider repeat CXR in 6 weeks if persistent symptoms (ensure pneumonia cleared, r/o underlying mass in smoker).

COPD symptom control (stable breathing, minimal rescue inhaler use <2x/week, able to perform ADLs without significant dyspnea) - Timeframe: 2-4 weeks. Strategies: Complete prednisone taper, continue maintenance inhalers (Advair, Combivent), oxygen therapy, pulmonary rehabilitation. Monitoring: Dyspnea scale (Borg scale or MRC scale), rescue inhaler use frequency, SpO2 with activity, nighttime symptoms. Follow-up: Pulmonology in 3 weeks (assess exacerbation resolution, adjust inhaler regimen if needed, schedule PFTs).

Maintain smoking abstinence (no cigarettes during or after hospitalization stress) - Timeframe: Ongoing, high priority during hospitalization stress period. Strategies: Nicotine replacement (patch 21mg daily if cravings develop), behavioral support (hospital social work, quitline enrollment), stress management (deep breathing, distraction techniques), involve family support. Monitoring: Daily assessment of cravings, triggers, mood (depression/anxiety can trigger relapse). Follow-up: Smoking cessation counseling at PCP visit, consider varenicline or bupropion if high relapse risk.

Functional mobility improvement (independent ambulation with walker, able to perform ADLs with minimal assistance, return to baseline functional status) - Timeframe: 4-6 weeks. Strategies: PT/OT home health (2-3x/week), daily exercises (provided by PT), gradual activity progression, energy conservation techniques, adequate rest. Monitoring: Ambulation distance (goal: 500 feet with walker), ADL independence (dressing, bathing, toileting), fall risk assessment. Follow-up: PT reassessment at 4 weeks, transition to outpatient PT if further therapy needed or discharge if goals met.

Glycemic control on prednisone taper (prevent hyperglycemia from steroid therapy, maintain blood glucose <200 mg/dL) - Timeframe: 7 days (duration of prednisone taper). Strategies: Increase BG monitoring (fasting + pre-dinner), may need temporary insulin dose increase (call PCP if BG consistently >200), maintain diabetic diet, adequate hydration. Monitoring: BG log (fasting, pre-dinner, bedtime if >250), symptoms of hyperglycemia (increased thirst, urination, blurred vision). Follow-up: PCP visit in 1 week (review BG log, adjust insulin if needed), return to baseline insulin dose after prednisone completed.

Safe discharge to home with adequate support (home health services in place, DME delivered, caregiver education completed, follow-up appointments scheduled) - Timeframe: Day of discharge. Coordination: Case management arranged home health (PT, OT, nursing for medication teaching), DME company delivering oxygen concentrator/walker/commode, pharmacy filling discharge medications (ready for pickup), follow-up appointments scheduled (PCP 1 week, Pulmonology 3 weeks), emergency contact numbers provided (PCP office, pulmonology, hospital, 911). Caregiver: Daughter will stay with patient x 1 week (assist with ADLs, medication management, monitor for complications).
input.provider: Dr. Sarah Mitchell, MD - Pulmonology (attending physician during hospitalization) & Dr. Robert Chen, MD - Primary Care (outpatient follow-up)
input.specialty: Pulmonology / Critical Care Medicine / Internal Medicine / Primary Care

immediateInterventions.monitoring: Daily weight (report gain >2 lbs/day or >5 lbs/week - fluid overload), daily blood glucose (fasting + pre-dinner while on prednisone, report >250 mg/dL), oxygen saturation monitoring (maintain SpO2 >92% on 2L O2, report <90%), temperature (report fever >100.4°F - infection), respiratory rate/dyspnea assessment (report increased shortness of breath, use of accessory muscles, unable to speak full sentences), sputum color/amount (report yellow-green purulent sputum - infection), pedal edema (report worsening lower extremity swelling)
immediateInterventions.equipment: Home oxygen concentrator (2L continuous via nasal cannula, portable O2 tank for outings), pulse oximeter (check SpO2 BID and with activity), walker (standard walker for ambulation safety), raised toilet seat (reduce energy expenditure), shower chair (conserve energy during bathing), bedside commode (nighttime use to reduce fall risk), nebulizer machine (for ipratropium/albuterol treatments if MDI technique difficult), diabetic glucometer + testing strips (BG monitoring)
immediateInterventions.patientEducation: COPD exacerbation warning signs: Increased dyspnea, increased sputum production, change in sputum color (yellow-green), fever, chest tightness, increased cough, decreased exercise tolerance, confusion (hypoxia). Pneumonia prevention: Hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts, annual flu vaccine, pneumococcal vaccine (age 65+), good oral hygiene. Smoking cessation: Remain abstinent (quit 2 years ago), avoid triggers, nicotine replacement if cravings, support group enrollment. Diabetes management: Medication adherence, blood glucose monitoring, foot care, annual eye exams. When to call doctor: Fever >100.4°F, worsening dyspnea not relieved by rescue inhaler, chest pain, SpO2 <90%, unable to eat/drink, confusion, swelling in legs. When to call 911: Severe difficulty breathing, chest pain with radiation, altered mental status, lips/fingernails turning blue.

pendingProcedures: Pulmonary function testing (PFTs) - outpatient in 6 weeks after acute exacerbation resolves (assess COPD severity, FEV1, establish baseline)
Chest CT scan - outpatient in 3 months if symptoms not fully resolved (r/o underlying structural lung disease, bronchiectasis, malignancy given smoking history)
Echocardiogram - outpatient in 2 months (assess for pulmonary hypertension from COPD, evaluate LV function given diabetes + HTN)
Colonoscopy - OVERDUE (age 68, last at 58 - 10 years ago, due for repeat). Schedule when acute illness resolved (2-3 months).
Diabetic eye exam - outpatient ophthalmology (dilated retinal exam for diabetic retinopathy screening, last exam 18 months ago - overdue)

getMedicationOptimization.description: Retrieve medication optimization records for a patient

input.type: Comprehensive Medication Optimization Analysis - Polypharmacy Reduction & Adherence Enhancement
input.provider: Dr. Emily Martinez, PharmD, BCPS - Clinical Pharmacy Specialist & Dr. Robert Chen, MD - Primary Care
input.facility: Memorial Hospital Clinical Pharmacy Services + Primary Care Clinic
input.findings:
78-year-old female currently on 15 medications (POLYPHARMACY - high risk for adverse drug events, falls, non-adherence). CURRENT REGIMEN: (1) Aspirin 81mg daily, (2) Atorvastatin 40mg daily, (3) Lisinopril 20mg daily, (4) Metoprolol succinate 50mg daily, (5) Furosemide 40mg daily, (6) Potassium chloride 20mEq daily, (7) Metformin 1000mg BID, (8) Glipizide 10mg BID, (9) Omeprazole 20mg daily, (10) Calcium carbonate 500mg TID, (11) Vitamin D3 2000 IU daily, (12) Multivitamin daily, (13) Docusate 100mg BID, (14) Senna 2 tabs qHS PRN constipation, (15) Acetaminophen 650mg q6h PRN pain.

PILL BURDEN ANALYSIS:
• Total daily pills: 22 pills/day (8 AM, 2 lunch, 8 PM, 4 PRN)
• Multiple dosing times: BID medications require lunch dosing (patient often forgets)
• Patient reports difficulty swallowing large pills (potassium chloride 20mEq tabs)
• Cost: $420/month out-of-pocket (patient on fixed income - Social Security $1,200/month)

DRUG-DRUG INTERACTIONS IDENTIFIED:
• MODERATE: Lisinopril + Potassium chloride → Risk of hyperkalemia (K+ 5.1 mEq/L on recent labs - HIGH NORMAL)
• MODERATE: Metformin + contrast dye → Lactic acidosis risk (patient scheduled for CT with contrast in 2 weeks)
• MODERATE: Aspirin + Furosemide → Reduced diuretic efficacy
• MINOR: Calcium carbonate + Levothyroxine (if added) → Reduced absorption

THERAPEUTIC DUPLICATION:
• Docusate + Senna - BOTH laxatives (constipation likely from calcium carbonate, not primary issue)
• Calcium carbonate TID + Multivitamin (contains 200mg calcium) → Total 1700mg/day (excessive, >1200mg recommended for elderly)

SUBOPTIMAL DOSING:
• Glipizide 10mg BID - HIGH dose for elderly (hypoglycemia risk, especially with declining renal function eGFR 52)
• Furosemide 40mg daily + inadequate potassium replacement (K+ borderline high despite supplementation - suggests poor adherence or inappropriate dosing)

ADHERENCE BARRIERS IDENTIFIED:
• Patient self-reports missing PM doses 3-4x/week (too many pills, forgets evening medications)
• Confusion about PRN medications (takes acetaminophen and senna daily even when not needed)
• Cost burden: Skips Atorvastatin every other day to "make it last longer" (non-adherence to statin)
• No medication organizer - patient uses original pill bottles (error-prone)

QUALITY OF LIFE IMPACT:
• Patient describes feeling like "all I do is take pills" - medication burden affecting daily activities
• Avoids social outings due to need to take lunch medications
• Constipation from calcium carbonate limiting mobility

FUNCTIONAL/COGNITIVE ASSESSMENT:
• MMSE: 26/30 (mild cognitive impairment) - affects medication management ability
• Vision: Wears glasses, difficulty reading small pill bottle labels
• Dexterity: Mild arthritis - struggles opening child-resistant caps
• Support: Lives alone, daughter visits 2x/week to help with medications
input.assessment:
HIGH-PRIORITY MEDICATION OPTIMIZATION CASE - Multiple opportunities for regimen simplification, cost reduction, and adherence enhancement:

1️⃣ POLYPHARMACY REDUCTION (15 → TARGET 10-12 medications):
   • Current: 15 medications, 22 pills/day, 4 dosing times
   • GOAL: Reduce to ≤12 medications, ≤15 pills/day, 2 dosing times (AM/PM only)
   • RATIONALE: Every additional medication ↑ falls risk 10%, adverse drug events, non-adherence
   • STRATEGY: Discontinue therapeutic duplications, unnecessary medications, switch to combination products

2️⃣ ADHERENCE IMPROVEMENT:
   • CURRENT ADHERENCE: ~70% (self-report, pharmacy refill data shows missed refills)
   • GOAL: >90% adherence
   • BARRIERS: Pill burden (22/day), cost ($420/month), complexity (BID dosing), cognitive impairment (MMSE 26)
   • SOLUTIONS: Simplify regimen (QD dosing), reduce cost (generics, assistance programs), medication organizer, caregiver involvement

3️⃣ DRUG INTERACTION MANAGEMENT:
   • Lisinopril + K+ supplement → Hyperkalemia risk (K+ 5.1 - high normal)
   • ACTION: Discontinue K+ supplement, monitor K+ (ACE inhibitor alone may be sufficient)
   • Metformin + CT contrast (scheduled 2 weeks) → Lactic acidosis risk
   • ACTION: HOLD Metformin 48 hours before and after contrast, recheck creatinine before restarting

4️⃣ COST REDUCTION:
   • Current: $420/month (35% of Social Security income)
   • GOAL: <$100/month (<10% income)
   • OPPORTUNITIES: Switch branded to generics, eliminate unnecessary meds (save $200/month), patient assistance programs for Atorvastatin

5️⃣ HYPOGLYCEMIA RISK REDUCTION:
   • Glipizide 10mg BID in elderly patient with CKD (eGFR 52) - HIGH RISK
   • Recent A1c 6.8% (well-controlled) - opportunity to deprescribe or reduce dose
   • SAFER ALTERNATIVES: Reduce Glipizide dose, or discontinue and rely on Metformin alone (if sufficient)

6️⃣ MEDICATION APPROPRIATENESS:
   • PPI (Omeprazole) - indication unclear, on chronic therapy (>1 year)
   • Calcium carbonate TID - excessive dosing, causing constipation (requiring 2 laxatives)
   • PRN medications used daily - patient confusion about appropriate use

recommendations.recommendation: DISCONTINUE Potassium chloride 20mEq daily. RATIONALE: K+ 5.1 (high normal), Lisinopril provides K+-sparing effect, interaction risk. Monitor: Recheck K+ in 2 weeks (goal 4.0-4.5). Savings: $15/month.

DISCONTINUE Glipizide 10mg BID. RATIONALE: A1c 6.8% over-controlled, hypoglycemia risk in elderly with CKD. Continue Metformin alone. Monitor: A1c in 12 weeks (goal 7.0-7.5%), home glucose BID x 1 week. Savings: $25/month, eliminate 2 pills/day.

REDUCE Calcium carbonate from 500mg TID to 500mg daily with largest meal. RATIONALE: Excessive dosing causing constipation. Monitor: Constipation improvement. Savings: $8/month, eliminate 2 pills/day.

DISCONTINUE Docusate 100mg BID after calcium reduction. RATIONALE: Constipation likely calcium-induced. Trial off laxatives. CONTINUE Senna PRN only (if no BM x 3 days). Savings: $10/month, eliminate 2 pills/day.
DISCONTINUE Multivitamin. RATIONALE: Therapeutic duplication (calcium, vitamin D already supplemented). Continue Vitamin D3 2000 IU daily for bone health. Savings: $12/month, eliminate 1 pill/day.

DISCONTINUE Omeprazole 20mg daily (trial off). RATIONALE: Indication unclear, chronic use >1 year increases fracture/C.diff risk. Monitor for GERD symptoms x 4 weeks. If symptoms: Restart or use PRN famotidine. Savings: $18/month.
REDUCE Furosemide from 40mg to 20mg daily (trial). Monitor: Daily weights (report >2 lbs gain), BP, recheck BMP in 2 weeks. If inadequate diuresis: Return to 40mg.

HOLD Metformin 48 hours BEFORE and AFTER CT scan with contrast (scheduled 2 weeks). Recheck creatinine before restarting. RATIONALE: Prevent contrast-induced lactic acidosis. Patient to mark calendar.
Provide large-compartment 7-day AM/PM medication organizer (MedCenter XL). Daughter to fill weekly. Eliminate all lunch dosing (major adherence barrier). Simplify to AM/PM only regimen.
Apply for Atorvastatin Patient Assistance Program (potential $100/month savings). Enroll in 90-day mail order pharmacy (additional 10% savings). Target total cost <$100/month.
Medication synchronization - align all refills to same monthly date. Single pharmacy visit/month vs 3-4 currently. Improves adherence and reduces patient burden.
Phone follow-up in 1 week: Adherence check, confusion about discontinued meds, GERD symptoms after stopping PPI, constipation improvement after reducing calcium.
Lab work in 2 weeks (before CT): BMP (K+ goal 4.0-4.5 after D/C supplement, creatinine for metformin), fasting glucose (monitor after stopping Glipizide).
Primary care follow-up in 4 weeks: Adherence assessment (pill counts), BP/weight (monitor Furosemide reduction), review home glucose log, assess GERD symptoms.
Labs in 12 weeks: A1c (goal 7.0-7.5% on Metformin alone), lipid panel (Atorvastatin adherence), BMP (K+, creatinine stability).

results.optimizedMedications: 11 medications (27% reduction), 14 pills/day (36% reduction), 2 dosing times (AM/PM only)
results.hypoglycemiaRiskReduction: HIGH→LOW (D/C Glipizide, A1c goal 7.0-7.5% vs 6.8% over-controlled)
results.fallRiskReduction: 15 meds→11 meds (10% per-medication fall risk reduction = 40% total risk reduction)
results.qualityOfLifeImpact: Simplified regimen (no lunch dosing), reduced pill burden, lower cost burden (35%→<10% income)

input.status: ACTIVE OPTIMIZATION PLAN - Initiated 11/25/2024. Reduced from 15→11 medications (27% reduction), 22→14 pills/day (36% reduction), eliminated lunch dosing, cost reduced $420→$232/month (target <$100 with assistance). Close monitoring: Phone 1 week, labs 2 weeks, PCP 4 weeks, labs 12 weeks. Expected outcomes: Adherence 70%→>90%, reduced fall/hypoglycemia risk, improved QOL, cost savings.

input.type: Comprehensive Preventive Care Gap Analysis - Multi-Domain Screening & Vaccination Review
input.provider: Dr. Jennifer Martinez, MD - Internal Medicine & Quality Improvement Committee

screenings.actionRequired: Schedule colonoscopy URGENT - 2+ years overdue (last colonoscopy 2012 at age 58, normal with 2 small hyperplastic polyps). Patient at average risk for colon cancer, guideline recommends 10-year interval. Discussed with patient today - she reports prior scheduling barrier (transportation issue), now resolved. GI referral placed today for colonoscopy scheduling within 4-6 weeks.

Schedule screening mammogram - 12 months overdue (last mammogram November 2022, BI-RADS 1 normal). Patient has family history of breast cancer (mother diagnosed age 65, sister diagnosed age 58 with BRCA-negative). Annual screening recommended per USPSTF guidelines. Patient reports scheduling conflict last year - willing to schedule today. Radiology order placed for bilateral screening mammogram, patient to call for appointment.

Schedule dilated eye exam with ophthalmology - 11 months overdue (last exam December 2022 showed mild non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy NPDR, no macular edema). Patient has Type 2 Diabetes x 12 years (current HbA1c 7.3%), hypertension (BP today 138/84), hyperlipidemia (LDL 95 mg/dL on statin). ADA guidelines recommend annual eye exam for patients with diabetic retinopathy. Ophthalmology referral placed today for dilated exam within 4 weeks to assess retinopathy progression.

ADMINISTER Pneumococcal PCV20 vaccine TODAY - Patient age 68 (≥65 years) with COPD (chronic lung disease) + diabetes (high-risk conditions). CDC ACIP 2022 guidelines recommend PCV20 single dose for adults ≥65 OR high-risk adults 19-64. Patient reports NO prior pneumococcal vaccine (PCV13 or PPSV23) per chart review. PCV20 preferred (covers 20 serotypes, no PPSV23 booster needed). Patient consent obtained, no contraindications. Vaccine administered today 11/25/2024 (left deltoid).

ADMINISTER Influenza vaccine TODAY - Annual flu vaccine recommended for all adults, especially high-risk patients (COPD, diabetes, age ≥65). Patient reports NO flu shot this season (current season 2024-2025 started October 2024). High-dose or adjuvanted vaccine preferred for age ≥65 per CDC guidelines. Patient consent obtained. Fluzone High-Dose Quadrivalent (0.7mL IM) administered today 11/25/2024 (right deltoid). Educated on common side effects (arm soreness, mild fatigue).

Schedule DEXA scan - 2+ years overdue (last DEXA June 2020 showed osteopenia T-score lumbar spine -1.8, femoral neck -1.5). Patient is 68-year-old female (postmenopausal x 18 years), risk factors: low BMI 19.2 (underweight), COPD on chronic corticosteroids (prednisone 10mg daily x 5 years = high fracture risk), smoking history (quit 2020, 40 pack-years). USPSTF guidelines recommend repeat DEXA every 2 years for osteopenia with risk factors. Radiology order placed for DEXA lumbar spine + bilateral hips, patient to schedule within 4 weeks. Will consider bisphosphonate therapy if progression to osteoporosis.

HbA1c completed 11/15/2024 - result 7.3% (above ADA goal <7.0% for most adults with diabetes). Next HbA1c due February 2025 (3 months). Continue current diabetes regimen: Metformin 1000mg BID, Glipizide 10mg daily. Discussed lifestyle modifications: carbohydrate counting, 150 min/week moderate exercise. If HbA1c remains >7.0% in February, consider adding GLP-1 RA (e.g., semaglutide) per ADA Standards of Care 2024.

Lipid panel completed 11/01/2024 - LDL 95 mg/dL (at goal <100 for ASCVD risk), HDL 48 mg/dL, TG 145 mg/dL. Patient on Atorvastatin 40mg daily for primary prevention (10-year ASCVD risk 18% per ACC/AHA calculator). Next lipid panel due May 2025 (6 months). Continue statin therapy, reinforce Mediterranean diet, plant stanols/sterols supplementation.

input.assessment:
COMPREHENSIVE PREVENTIVE CARE GAP ASSESSMENT - High-Risk Patient with Multiple Overdue Screenings:

🎯 GAP SEVERITY ANALYSIS:

1️⃣ CRITICAL CANCER SCREENING GAPS (2 items - HIGHEST PRIORITY):
   • Colonoscopy overdue 2+ years → Colon cancer risk (age 68, polyp history)
   • Mammogram overdue 12 months → Breast cancer risk (strong family history)
   • IMPACT: Delayed cancer detection, potentially missed early-stage treatable disease
   • ROOT CAUSE: Access barriers (transportation, scheduling conflicts)

2️⃣ DIABETES COMPLICATION MONITORING GAP (1 item - HIGH PRIORITY):
   • Diabetic eye exam overdue 11 months → Retinopathy progression risk
   • Baseline: Mild NPDR (2022) → Unknown current status
   • Risk factors: Suboptimal glycemic control (HbA1c 7.3%), hypertension, hyperlipidemia
   • IMPACT: Potential vision loss if retinopathy progresses undetected

3️⃣ INFECTION PREVENTION GAPS (2 items - ADDRESSED TODAY):
   • Pneumococcal vaccine missing → Pneumonia/sepsis risk (5-7x higher with COPD/diabetes)
   • Influenza vaccine overdue → Flu complications risk (hospitalization, COPD exacerbation)
   • IMPACT: Preventable serious infections in high-risk patient
   • RESOLUTION: Both vaccines ADMINISTERED TODAY (11/25/2024)

4️⃣ BONE HEALTH MONITORING GAP (1 item - MEDIUM PRIORITY):
   • DEXA scan overdue 2+ years → Osteoporosis progression risk
   • Risk: Chronic corticosteroids (prednisone 10mg daily x 5 years), low BMI, age 68
   • IMPACT: Undetected bone loss, fracture risk (hip/spine fractures in elderly)

📊 QUALITY METRICS IMPACT:
   • HEDIS measures affected: Colorectal Cancer Screening, Breast Cancer Screening, Diabetes Eye Exam, Pneumococcal Vaccination (age ≥65)
   • Patient at risk for quality gaps affecting practice performance metrics

🔍 SYSTEMIC ISSUES IDENTIFIED:
   • Access barriers: Transportation (colonoscopy), scheduling conflicts (mammogram)
   • Patient engagement: Willing to complete screenings when barriers addressed
   • Care coordination: Multiple specialty referrals needed (GI, radiology, ophthalmology)

✅ POSITIVE FINDINGS:
   • Chronic disease monitoring on track: HbA1c, lipid panel current
   • Patient engaged and motivated during today's visit
   • Transportation barrier resolved (patient now has reliable access)

🎯 PRIORITY RANKING:
   1. URGENT (complete within 4-6 weeks): Colonoscopy, mammogram, diabetic eye exam
   2. HIGH (complete within 4 weeks): DEXA scan
   3. COMPLETED TODAY: Pneumococcal vaccine, influenza vaccine

recommendations.recommendation: COLONOSCOPY URGENT - Schedule within 4-6 weeks via GI referral (Dr. Chen). Overdue 2+ years (last 2012). Transportation arranged with daughter. GI office will call patient for scheduling.
MAMMOGRAM - Schedule within 4-6 weeks. Call Memorial Hospital Breast Imaging 555-9729. Annual screening recommended (family history breast cancer). Last mammogram November 2022 normal.
DIABETIC EYE EXAM - Ophthalmology referral placed (Dr. Wong). Schedule within 4 weeks. Dilated exam to assess diabetic retinopathy progression (baseline mild NPDR 2022, overdue 11 months).

DEXA SCAN - Schedule within 4 weeks. Call Memorial Radiology 555-9729. Assess osteoporosis progression (baseline osteopenia 2020, high fracture risk with chronic prednisone). Results review December 20, 2024 visit.
PNEUMOCOCCAL VACCINE (PCV20) - COMPLETED TODAY 11/25/2024. No booster needed. Lifelong protection against 20 serotypes. Patient educated on side effects (arm soreness, fatigue).
INFLUENZA VACCINE (Fluzone High-Dose) - COMPLETED TODAY 11/25/2024. Next annual flu shot due October 2025. Patient educated on expected side effects (arm soreness 24-48 hours).
CARE COORDINATION FOLLOW-UP - Medical assistant to call patient in 2 weeks to verify all 4 screening appointments scheduled. Provide scheduling assistance if barriers encountered.
NEXT VISIT December 20, 2024 - Review DEXA results, verify screening completion status, discuss bisphosphonate therapy if osteoporosis detected.

gapsByCategory.monitoring: 3 items (diabetic eye exam - gap, HbA1c - on track, lipid panel - on track)

results.patientEngagement: High - patient motivated, verbalized understanding, committed to scheduling all appointments within 2 weeks
results.nextReviewDate: 2024-12-20 (follow-up visit to assess gap closure progress)

input.status: ACTIVE - Preventive Care Gap Closure in Progress. 2/6 gaps closed today (vaccines administered). 4 gaps pending patient scheduling (colonoscopy, mammogram, eye exam, DEXA) - referrals/orders placed. MA follow-up call scheduled 12/9/2024 to verify appointments. Next review 12/20/2024.

input.indication: Evaluation of dysphagia and suspected gastric ulcer. Patient reports 3-month history of progressive difficulty swallowing solids and epigastric pain.

input.findings: Esophagus: Normal mucosa, no strictures or masses. Stomach: 1.5 cm ulcer in gastric antrum at lesser curvature, clean base, no active bleeding. Biopsies obtained x2 from ulcer margin. Duodenum: Normal mucosa, no ulcers or masses. H. pylori rapid urease test: Positive.
input.technique: Patient placed in left lateral decubitus position. Conscious sedation with midazolam 2mg IV and fentanyl 50mcg IV. Olympus GIF-180 gastroscope advanced through oropharynx under direct visualization. Esophagus, stomach, and proximal duodenum examined systematically. Cold forceps biopsies obtained from gastric ulcer margin. Scope withdrawn without difficulty. Patient tolerated procedure well.
input.complications: None. No bleeding, perforation, or adverse reaction to sedation.
input.outcome: Procedure completed successfully. Gastric ulcer identified and biopsied. H. pylori infection confirmed.
specimens: Gastric antrum biopsy x2 (sent to pathology)
input.anesthesia: Conscious sedation: Midazolam 2mg IV, Fentanyl 50mcg IV. Patient monitored with continuous pulse oximetry and BP monitoring. Recovery uneventful.
input.notes: Post-procedure: Patient recovered in PACU for 30 minutes, vital signs stable, discharged home with responsible adult. Discharge instructions given regarding diet restrictions (NPO 2 hours, then clear liquids). Pathology results pending. Patient started on omeprazole 40mg daily and amoxicillin/clarithromycin for H. pylori eradication. Follow-up EGD in 8 weeks to confirm ulcer healing.

input.plan: 1. Admit to telemetry for serial troponins and continuous monitoring. 2. Start dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin 325mg and clopidogrel 300mg loading dose. 3. Continue atorvastatin 80mg daily. 4. Schedule cardiac catheterization within 24 hours. 5. Cardiology to follow throughout hospitalization.

Cardiac rehabilitation referral post-procedure
Lipid panel recheck in 6 weeks with goal LDL <70
Follow-up in cardiology clinic in 2 weeks post-discharge

input.admittingDiagnosis: Acute chest pain, rule out myocardial infarction
input.hospitalCourse: Patient is a 68-year-old male admitted with acute substernal chest pain radiating to left arm. EKG showed ST depressions in leads V4-V6. Troponin elevated at 2.4. Cardiology consulted. Patient underwent cardiac catheterization on day 2 revealing 85% LAD stenosis. Successful PCI with drug-eluting stent placement performed. Post-procedure course uncomplicated. Blood glucose managed with insulin. Blood pressure controlled with medication adjustments. Patient ambulating without chest pain by day 5.
proceduresPerformed: Percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) with drug-eluting stent to LAD
input.dischargeCondition: Stable, improved, chest pain resolved, ambulating independently
input.dischargeInstructions: Take all medications as prescribed. Monitor blood pressure daily and keep log. Check blood glucose twice daily. Call 911 for any chest pain, shortness of breath, or concerning symptoms. No heavy lifting over 10 pounds for 2 weeks. Gradually increase activity as tolerated. Attend cardiac rehabilitation.
followUpAppointments: Cardiology follow-up with Dr. Martinez in 2 weeks on 2024-02-05
Primary care with Dr. Johnson in 1 week on 2024-01-29
input.activityRestrictions: No heavy lifting over 10 pounds for 2 weeks, no driving for 1 week, gradually resume normal activities, start cardiac rehabilitation program
input.dietRestrictions: Cardiac diet - low sodium (2g daily), low saturated fat, diabetic diet with carbohydrate counting, limit cholesterol intake
input.electronicSignature: Electronically signed by Dr. Robert Thompson, MD on 2024-01-22 at 14:35

getMedicationSafetyAlerts.description: Retrieve medication safety alerts records for a patient

input.provider: Dr. Sarah Chen, PharmD, Clinical Pharmacy Specialist
input.facility: Memorial Regional Medical Center - Main Campus
input.findings: Patient received 10mg morphine IV instead of prescribed 2mg dose due to miscommunication during shift change. Near-miss intercepted by recovery room nurse before administration of second dose. IV pump programmed incorrectly. Patient exhibited increased sedation and respiratory rate decreased to 8 breaths/min.
input.assessment: Medication administration error involving 5-fold overdose of high-alert opioid medication. Contributing factors include inadequate verbal handoff communication, failure to verify pump settings independently, and missing hard stop alert in CPOE system for weight-based dosing limits.
input.plan: Immediate naloxone administration protocol initiated. Patient transferred to ICU for continuous monitoring. Root cause analysis to be completed within 48 hours. Pharmacy to implement enhanced barcode verification for all opioid administrations.

recommendations.text: Implement mandatory two-nurse verification for all IV opioid administration
Add hard stop alert in CPOE for morphine doses exceeding 5mg without pain specialist approval
Conduct unit-wide in-service training on high-alert medication protocols
Update IV pump drug library with stricter dose limits for opioids

results.patient_outcome: Stable after naloxone, no permanent harm

input.notes: This is the third opioid-related safety event this quarter. Pharmacy director has escalated to hospital safety committee. Family notified and full disclosure provided per institutional policy. Event reported to state health department as required.
input.status: Under Investigation - Root Cause Analysis Pending

getProposedArtSwitch.description: Retrieve proposed art switch records for a patient

input.type: ART regimen modification due to virologic failure
input.findings: Patient on TDF/FTC/EFV for 18 months. Recent viral load 8,500 copies/mL (previously undetectable). CD4 count 310 cells/µL (down from 485). Genotype resistance testing shows K103N and M184V mutations. Patient reports 85% adherence, occasional missed weekend doses. No current opportunistic infections.
input.assessment: Virologic failure on current NNRTI-based regimen secondary to documented resistance mutations and suboptimal adherence. M184V confers high-level resistance to 3TC/FTC. K103N confers resistance to EFV and NVP. CD4 decline consistent with virologic failure. Patient appropriate candidate for switch to integrase inhibitor-based regimen.
input.plan: Discontinue TDF/FTC/EFV. Initiate Biktarvy (BIC/TAF/FTC) one tablet daily. Repeat viral load in 4 weeks, then 12 weeks. Monitor renal function and bone density given switch from TDF to TAF. Adherence counseling scheduled with pharmacist.

recommendations.recommendation: Switch to BIC/TAF/FTC (Biktarvy) one tablet daily
Viral load repeat in 4 weeks
Adherence counseling with HIV pharmacist within 1 week
Recheck viral load, CD4, CMP, and hepatic panel

input.notes: Patient expressing motivation to improve adherence. Financial barriers to medication access resolved with patient assistance program. Partner also HIV positive and virally suppressed. Will coordinate care with case manager for medication delivery reminders.

getPrepAndDrape.description: Retrieve prep and drape records for a patient

input.prepSolution: Look in prep documentation for specific antiseptic solution brand and concentration. Common terms: ChloraPrep, Betadine, Hibiclens, DuraPrep, povidone-iodine, chlorhexidine, alcohol-based. Include concentration percentages if documented.
input.prepMethod: Identify application technique in nursing or circulating nurse notes. Look for: scrub technique, paint method, spray application, applicator stick, friction scrub, concentric circles pattern.
input.hairRemoval: Check pre-op and prep notes for hair removal documentation. Extract method: clipping, shaving, depilatory cream, or "none" if no hair removal performed. Note timing if mentioned (pre-op vs OR).
input.drapingMethod: Identify draping sequence and technique. Look for: four-corner towel technique, standard draping sequence, towel clip placement, order of drape application, specialty draping methods.
input.inciseDrape: Check for adhesive incise drape or Ioban use. Extract "Yes" with brand name (3M Ioban, Steri-Drape, VI-Drape) or "No" if not used. Note any antimicrobial properties mentioned.
input.timeoutPerformed: Look in surgical safety checklist or time-out documentation. Extract "Yes" with time if documented, note if entire team participated. Include brief details like "timeout completed with all team members present.
input.sterilityVerified: Check for documentation of sterile field maintenance. Look for phrases like "sterility maintained," "no breaks in sterile technique," "sterile field intact." Extract yes/no and any relevant details.
input.notes: Capture all additional prep-related details: patient allergies affecting prep choice, skin condition pre-prep, prep complications, extended prep areas for potential conversion, special circumstances, deviations from standard protocol.

input.drains: Record any drains placed including type (Jackson-Pratt, Blake, Penrose), size (French or mm), location; if no drains, state "No drains placed
input.tensionFree: Find documentation of tension assessment; look for "tension-free closure", "adequate tissue approximation without tension", "no undue tension
input.facility: Capture hospital or surgical center name from document header or facility identification section

input.interpretation: Abnormal EEG due to left temporal epileptiform activity and one electroclinical seizure of left temporal onset. Findings consistent with focal epilepsy of temporal lobe origin. Clinical correlation recommended.

getNeuropsychologicalAssessments.description: Retrieve neuropsychological assessments records for a patient

input.executiveFunction: Moderate impairment on Trail Making Test B (180 seconds, >2 SD below norm). Wisconsin Card Sorting Test shows perseverative errors (28%) indicating difficulty with set-shifting. Stroop Color-Word Test demonstrates intact inhibitory control.
input.verbalFluency: Phonemic fluency (FAS test): 24 words in 60 seconds (low average, 25th percentile). Semantic fluency (animals): 14 words (mildly impaired, 10th percentile). Category switching shows reduced flexibility.
input.processingSpeed: WAIS-IV Processing Speed Index: 78 (7th percentile). Symbol Search scaled score: 6. Coding scaled score: 5. Performance indicates marked slowing in visual-motor processing tasks.
input.memory: WMS-IV Auditory Memory Index: 82. Immediate recall 6/16 words, delayed recall 4/16. Visual memory relatively preserved (90th percentile). Working memory deficit evident on Digit Span backward (score: 4).
input.overallFindings: Neuropsychological profile consistent with frontal-subcortical dysfunction. Primary deficits in executive function, processing speed, and verbal fluency. Memory encoding difficulties secondary to attentional deficits. Relative preservation of visual-spatial skills and language comprehension.

comprehensiveTestBattery.testingConditions: Adequate effort, cooperative, no sensory limitations noted

cognitiveDomainsDetailed.attention: Sustained attention impaired on CPT-3 (omission errors 18%). Divided attention significantly compromised.
cognitiveDomainsDetailed.language: Boston Naming Test: 52/60 (intact). Comprehension preserved. Confrontation naming mildly reduced.
cognitiveDomainsDetailed.visuospatialSkills: Rey Complex Figure copy: 32/36 (preserved). Block Design scaled score: 11 (average range).
cognitiveDomainsDetailed.abstractReasoning: Similarities scaled score: 8 (low average). Concrete thinking noted on proverb interpretation.

prePostComparison.executiveFunctionChange: Trail Making B improved from 220 sec to 180 sec (19% improvement)
prePostComparison.memoryChange: Auditory Memory Index increased from 76 to 82 (clinical significance borderline)
prePostComparison.processingSpeedChange: Processing Speed Index stable (79 to 78, no significant change)
prePostComparison.overallTrajectory: Mild improvements in executive function, plateau in processing speed, ongoing memory deficits

functionalImplications.dailyLivingActivities: Requires supervision for medication management, bill paying, and complex meal preparation. Independent in basic ADLs.
functionalImplications.workCapacity: Currently unable to return to previous position as accountant due to processing speed and executive deficits. May benefit from supported employment with reduced cognitive demands.
functionalImplications.drivingRecommendation: On-road evaluation recommended before return to driving due to slowed processing and divided attention deficits.
functionalImplications.socialFunctioning: Family reports increased social withdrawal and difficulty following group conversations.

goals: Return to part-time work within 6 months
cognitiveRehabilitationPlan.compensatoryStrategies: Daily planner use, smartphone reminders, task breakdown for complex activities, reduced multitasking

diagnosisImplications.cognitiveProfile: Profile most consistent with vascular cognitive impairment affecting frontal-subcortical circuits
diagnosisImplications.differentialConsiderations: Pattern inconsistent with Alzheimer's dementia (memory encoding vs consolidation). Rule out depression contributing to processing speed deficits.
diagnosisImplications.consistencyWithNeuroimaging: Cognitive findings correlate with MRI showing periventricular white matter hyperintensities and lacunar infarcts in basal ganglia
diagnosisImplications.diagnosticImpression: Major Neurocognitive Disorder due to Vascular Disease, with deficits in executive function, processing speed, and complex attention (DSM-5 criteria met)

input.findings: 32-year-old female with severe Crohn's disease, Montreal classification A2L3B2, failed vedolizumab and adalimumab. Current CDAI score 285. CT enterography shows 15cm terminal ileum stricture with proximal dilation. Weight loss 18 lbs over 3 months. PHQ-9 score 14 indicating moderate depression. Albumin 2.8, CRP 45, fecal calprotectin 850.
input.assessment: Refractory ileocolonic Crohn's disease with fibrostenotic complications and malnutrition. Patient requires surgical evaluation for strictureplasty vs resection. Protein-calorie malnutrition related to disease activity and dietary restrictions. Moderate depression likely secondary to chronic disease burden and quality of life impact.
input.plan: 1) Initiate ustekinumab 390mg IV loading dose after TB screening; 2) Refer to colorectal surgery for stricture management discussion; 3) Nutrition: high-protein supplementation 2 cans/day, consider TPN if oral intake remains inadequate; 4) Psychology: initiate CBT for chronic illness adaptation; 5) Labs: CBC, CMP, iron panel in 2 weeks; 6) Repeat imaging in 8 weeks to assess stricture response

recommendations.recommendation: Start ustekinumab 390mg IV induction
High-protein oral supplementation 60g/day
Surgical consultation within 2 weeks
Weekly psychology sessions x 8 weeks
Follow-up CT enterography

results.laboratory: CRP 45 mg/L, ESR 52 mm/hr, Albumin 2.8 g/dL, Hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL
results.imaging: CT enterography: 15cm terminal ileum stricture with wall thickening and upstream dilation to 4.5cm
results.endoscopy: Colonoscopy 12/2023: deep linear ulcerations in terminal ileum, impassable stricture at 25cm

input.notes: Patient expressed concern about surgical options and fertility preservation. Discussed strictureplasty vs resection approaches with surgery team. Patient agrees with biologic trial before proceeding to surgery. Social work addressing insurance authorization for ustekinumab and FMLA paperwork for ongoing care. Dietitian provided low-residue meal planning guide. Next team conference scheduled in 4 weeks to review treatment response.

predictedPostoperativeFev1.adequateForSurgery: Borderline - consider additional cardiopulmonary exercise testing

dlcoComprehensive.interpretation: Mild reduction in DLCO, proportional to alveolar volume. After hemoglobin correction, low-normal range. No evidence of isolated gas exchange impairment.

flowVolumeLoop.findings: Consistent with lower airway obstruction. No fixed or variable upper airway obstruction. Good inspiratory effort.

bronchodilatorResponse.clinicalSignificance: Significant reversibility suggests asthma or asthmatic component to COPD

cardiopulmonaryExerciseTest.limitingFactor: Ventilatory limitation with reduced breathing reserve
cardiopulmonaryExerciseTest.interpretation: Moderate reduction in exercise capacity primarily limited by ventilatory mechanics. Adequate cardiovascular response. Suitable for moderate-risk surgery with optimization.

qualityAssessment.reproducibility: FEV1 within 100 mL, FVC within 150 mL

getPolypharmacyReviews.description: Retrieve polypharmacy reviews records for a patient

input.provider: Dr. Sarah Johnson, PharmD, Clinical Pharmacist
input.facility: St. Mary's Hospital Medication Therapy Management Clinic
input.findings: Patient currently on 14 medications including 3 antihypertensives (lisinopril 20mg, amlodipine 10mg, hydrochlorothiazide 25mg), 2 diabetic agents (metformin 1000mg BID, glipizide 10mg), statin, anticoagulant (warfarin 5mg), PPI, SSRI, benzodiazepine, and multiple supplements. Identified potential drug-drug interaction between warfarin and omeprazole. Patient reports dizziness and falls in past month. Pill burden: 23 pills daily.
input.assessment: High-risk polypharmacy with medication burden of 14 concurrent medications. Identified therapeutic duplication in antihypertensive regimen with excessive blood pressure lowering potentially contributing to orthostatic hypotension and fall risk. Drug-drug interaction between warfarin and omeprazole may affect INR stability. Benzodiazepine use in elderly patient increases fall risk. Anticholinergic burden score of 4 indicating cognitive impairment risk.
input.plan: Deprescribing protocol initiated. Taper hydrochlorothiazide over 2 weeks with blood pressure monitoring. Switch omeprazole to famotidine to reduce warfarin interaction. Initiate benzodiazepine taper schedule over 6 weeks. Consolidate vitamin D and calcium supplements to single combination product. Schedule follow-up in 2 weeks for blood pressure recheck and medication tolerance assessment.

recommendations.recommendation: Discontinue hydrochlorothiazide 25mg - taper by 12.5mg weekly
Replace omeprazole 20mg with famotidine 20mg BID to reduce warfarin interaction
Initiate alprazolam taper: reduce from 0.5mg TID to 0.5mg BID for 2 weeks, then continue taper
Monitor INR weekly x 3 weeks after PPI change
Home blood pressure monitoring BID during medication adjustments

input.notes: Patient very receptive to deprescribing discussion. Caregiver daughter present and supportive. Patient expressed concern about dizziness interfering with daily activities. Will coordinate with PCP Dr. Matthews regarding medication changes. Provided written deprescribing schedule and medication list. Patient verbalized understanding of taper protocols.

getAutopsyReports.description: Retrieve autopsy reports records for a patient

input.indication: forensic
input.externalExamination: Well-developed, well-nourished adult male appearing stated age of 58 years. Body length 70 inches, weight 185 pounds. Rigor mortis present in all extremities. Livor mortis fixed and posterior. Abrasions noted on right forearm measuring 3x2cm. No defensive wounds identified. Conjunctival petechiae absent.
input.internalExamination: Body cavities opened via standard Y-incision. No adhesions noted. Organs in normal anatomic position. Approximately 400ml of serosanguinous fluid in right pleural cavity. Left pleural cavity contains 150ml similar fluid. Peritoneal cavity without significant fluid accumulation.
input.cardiovascular: Heart weighs 420 grams (normal range 270-360g for male). Moderate cardiomegaly present. Left ventricular wall thickness 1.8cm. Coronary arteries show 75% stenosis of left anterior descending artery, 60% stenosis of right coronary artery. No acute thrombosis. Myocardium shows patchy fibrosis consistent with old infarction in posterior wall.
input.respiratory: Right lung weighs 680 grams, left lung 620 grams. Bilateral pulmonary edema with frothy fluid in airways. Parenchyma congested and heavy. No masses or nodules identified. Pleural surfaces smooth except for focal adhesions at right apex. Trachea and bronchi patent without obstruction.
input.gastrointestinal: Esophagus patent without varices. Stomach contains approximately 200ml of partially digested food material with pill fragments. Gastric mucosa unremarkable. Small and large bowel without perforation, obstruction, or masses. Liver weighs 1850 grams, shows fatty change and cirrhotic nodularity. Gallbladder contains multiple small stones. Pancreas and spleen unremarkable.
input.neurologicalBrain: Brain weighs 1420 grams. Leptomeninges clear without subarachnoid hemorrhage. No subdural or epidural hematoma. Cerebral hemispheres symmetric. Coronal sections reveal no masses, infarcts, or hemorrhages. Mild cerebral edema present. Vessels at base of brain show moderate atherosclerosis. Cerebellum and brainstem unremarkable.
input.toxicology: Blood alcohol: 0.18 g/dL. Urine drug screen positive for benzodiazepines (alprazolam detected at 45ng/mL) and opioids (oxycodone 120ng/mL, morphine 15ng/mL). Negative for cocaine, amphetamines, cannabinoids, and barbiturates. Vitreous glucose: 85mg/dL.
input.microscopic: Heart: Sections show myocyte hypertrophy with interstitial fibrosis. Areas of old myocardial infarction with replacement fibrosis identified. Coronary arteries demonstrate severe atherosclerosis with calcification. Liver: Extensive macrovesicular steatosis involving >60% of hepatocytes. Bridging fibrosis consistent with advanced cirrhosis (stage 3-4). Lungs: Alveolar spaces filled with proteinaceous edema fluid. Hemosiderin-laden macrophages present.
input.causeOfDeath: Acute myocardial ischemia due to severe coronary artery atherosclerosis with cardiomegaly
input.pathologist: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD, Board Certified Forensic Pathologist
input.facility: County Medical Examiner's Office, Forensic Pathology Division
input.notes: Police report indicates decedent found unresponsive in bed by family member. EMS pronounced dead at scene. Prescription bottles for alprazolam and oxycodone found at bedside. Medical history significant for coronary artery disease, prior MI in 2019, alcohol use disorder. Case reviewed with toxicology consultant due to elevated blood alcohol and prescription drug levels.

input.indication: Refractory ascites secondary to hepatic cirrhosis with portal hypertension, Child-Pugh Class B
input.anatomicalSite: Right internal jugular vein to right hepatic vein to portal vein
input.imagingGuidance: Fluoroscopy with ultrasound guidance for initial venous access
input.access: Right internal jugular vein access obtained using ultrasound guidance with micropuncture technique, upgraded to 10 French sheath
input.procedureDetails: Under conscious sedation, right IJ access obtained. Catheter advanced to right hepatic vein. Portal vein accessed using Colapinto needle via transhepatic approach. Tract dilated and 10x8cm Viatorr stent deployed creating shunt between hepatic and portal veins. Post-deployment balloon angioplasty performed. Portal pressure reduced from 24mmHg to 12mmHg.
input.outcome: Technical success achieved with successful TIPS placement and pressure gradient reduction of 12mmHg. Adequate flow through shunt demonstrated on completion venography.
input.followUp: Doppler ultrasound in 24-48 hours to assess shunt patency, repeat ultrasound at 1 month and 3 months, hepatology follow-up in 2 weeks
input.notes: Patient tolerated procedure well. Hemostasis achieved at access site. Post-procedure hepatic encephalopathy prophylaxis initiated with lactulose.

getRadiologyReports.description: Retrieve radiology reports records for a patient

input.studyType: CT Abdomen and Pelvis with Contrast
input.indication: 67-year-old male with acute abdominal pain, fever, and elevated white blood cell count. Rule out appendicitis or diverticulitis.
input.technique: Helical CT imaging of the abdomen and pelvis performed on a 64-slice multidetector scanner. 5mm axial images reconstructed with 2.5mm coronal and sagittal reformats. IV contrast administered.
input.contrast: 100 mL Omnipaque 350 (iohexol) administered intravenously at 3 mL/sec via power injector. Portal venous phase imaging obtained at 70 seconds.
input.findings: The liver, spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands are unremarkable. Both kidneys enhance symmetrically without hydronephrosis or calculi. The appendix is not visualized. Sigmoid colon demonstrates wall thickening measuring up to 8mm with pericolonic fat stranding consistent with diverticulitis. Multiple diverticula noted throughout the sigmoid and descending colon. Small amount of free fluid in the pelvis. No pneumoperitoneum or abscess formation identified.
input.impression: Acute sigmoid diverticulitis with pericolonic inflammatory changes. No evidence of perforation or abscess. No appendicitis.

getPharmacyReview.description: Retrieve pharmacy review records for a patient

input.findings: Patient currently on 12 medications including metformin 1000mg BID, lisinopril 20mg daily, atorvastatin 40mg QHS, warfarin 5mg daily (INR 2.8), gabapentin 300mg TID. Duplicate therapy identified with ibuprofen 800mg TID and meloxicam 15mg daily. Drug-drug interaction noted between warfarin and recent fluconazole prescription. Patient reports dizziness, possibly related to lisinopril dose increase 2 weeks ago. Adherence concerns with evening medications.
input.assessment: 1. Polypharmacy with 12 active medications. 2. Duplicate NSAID therapy increases GI bleeding risk, especially with concurrent warfarin. 3. Significant drug interaction between warfarin and fluconazole requiring INR monitoring. 4. Potential adverse effect (orthostatic hypotension) from recent lisinopril titration. 5. Non-adherence to evening medication regimen due to complexity. 6. Suboptimal diabetes control based on recent A1C 8.2%.
input.plan: Discontinue meloxicam to eliminate duplicate NSAID therapy. Increase warfarin monitoring frequency to twice weekly during fluconazole course. Monitor blood pressure and hold lisinopril if SBP <100 or symptomatic hypotension. Simplify evening regimen by consolidating administration times. Schedule follow-up in 2 weeks for reassessment. Coordinate with prescribing physician regarding metformin dose adjustment.

recommendations.recommendation: Discontinue meloxicam 15mg daily - duplicate NSAID therapy
Increase INR monitoring to twice weekly during fluconazole therapy
Consider lisinopril dose reduction from 20mg to 10mg daily if symptomatic hypotension persists
Consolidate evening medications to single administration time (bedtime)
Follow-up pharmacy review in 2 weeks to assess adherence and symptom resolution

input.notes: Patient expressed frustration with medication complexity and cost burden. Provided education on warfarin-fluconazole interaction and importance of INR monitoring. Discussed NSAID risks with anticoagulation. Patient agreeable to medication changes. Communicated recommendations to Dr. Martinez via secure messaging. Patient provided with updated medication list and pill organizer setup instructions.

input.provider: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD - Medical Toxicologist
input.facility: Regional Poison Control Center - Memorial Hospital
input.findings: 43-year-old female presented with altered mental status 2 hours post-ingestion of approximately 50 tablets of acetaminophen 500mg (25g total). Patient exhibiting nausea, vomiting, diaphoresis. Initial vital signs: BP 145/92, HR 108, RR 22, Temp 37.8C. Physical exam notable for epigastric tenderness, drowsiness but arousable. No hepatomegaly on palpation.
input.assessment: Severe acute acetaminophen toxicity with high risk for fulminant hepatic failure. Rumack-Matthew nomogram indicates toxic level requiring immediate N-acetylcysteine therapy. Mental status changes consistent with early toxicity. Ingestion timeline and quantity confirmed by family witness.
input.plan: Immediate administration of N-acetylcysteine loading dose 150mg/kg IV over 60 minutes followed by standard 21-hour protocol. Serial acetaminophen levels q4h. Hepatic function panel, PT/INR, basic metabolic panel now and q12h. Activated charcoal contraindicated due to time since ingestion and vomiting. Psychiatric consultation for intentional ingestion. Admit to ICU for monitoring.

recommendations.text: Continue N-acetylcysteine infusion per protocol until acetaminophen level undetectable and transaminases trending down
Monitor for signs of hepatotoxicity including coagulopathy, encephalopathy, and rising transaminases
Repeat acetaminophen level at 24 hours post-ingestion to assess treatment response
Mandatory psychiatry evaluation before discharge given intentional ingestion

input.notes: Patient's family reports ongoing depression and recent job loss. This is her second overdose attempt in 6 months. Previous admission in July 2023 for benzodiazepine overdose. Patient expressed regret during lucid moments. Will require intensive psychiatric follow-up. Poison Control case number PCC-2024-00847. Follow-up call scheduled for 48 hours post-discharge.

input.plan: Optimize biologic therapy, recheck inflammatory markers in 8 weeks, continue iron supplementation for anemia, nutritional consultation for hypoalbuminemia, consider endoscopic evaluation if no improvement
recommendations: Increase adalimumab to weekly dosing
Repeat fecal calprotectin in 8 weeks
Continue oral iron 325mg daily
Nutrition consult for low albumin
Follow-up appointment in 6 weeks
input.notes: Patient reports increased stool frequency (8-10/day) with blood. Biomarkers correlate with clinical worsening. Previous calprotectin 6 months ago was 125 mcg/g on stable therapy. Discussed treatment escalation options with patient.

articular: peripheral arthropathy affecting bilateral knees and ankles
dermatologic: erythema nodosum on bilateral lower extremities
pyoderma gangrenosum 3cm ulcer left lateral leg
oral aphthous ulcers on buccal mucosa
ocular: episcleritis left eye with conjunctival injection
hepatobiliary: primary sclerosing cholangitis confirmed by MRCP
bile duct stricturing and beading pattern
pulmonary: restrictive pattern on PFTs with FVC 68% predicted
hematologic: anemia of chronic disease Hgb 9.2 g/dL
input.findings: Patient with known Crohn's disease presenting with multiple active extraintestinal manifestations including new-onset bilateral knee arthropathy, persistent erythema nodosum, and worsening PSC with cholestatic pattern. Physical exam reveals warm effusions bilateral knees, tender nodular lesions on shins, and scleral icterus. Imaging confirms sacroiliitis and bile duct changes.
input.assessment: Active Crohn's disease with multiple extraintestinal manifestations: 1) Peripheral arthropathy type 1 affecting large joints, 2) Erythema nodosum and pyoderma gangrenosum, 3) Progressive primary sclerosing cholangitis with cholestasis, 4) Recurrent anterior uveitis, 5) Renal stones likely secondary to malabsorption. These manifestations indicate systemic inflammatory activity requiring escalation of immunosuppression.
input.plan: 1) Initiate infliximab 5mg/kg for combined intestinal and extraintestinal disease control, 2) Continue ursodiol 15mg/kg for PSC, 3) Rheumatology co-management for arthropathy with consideration of intra-articular steroid injections, 4) Ophthalmology follow-up for uveitis monitoring, 5) Dermatology wound care for pyoderma gangrenosum, 6) Repeat LFTs and inflammatory markers in 4 weeks

recommendations.text: Start infliximab infusions q8weeks after negative TB screening
Increase ursodiol to 1200mg daily divided doses
Rheumatology evaluation within 2 weeks for joint assessment
Ophthalmology urgent referral for uveitis flare management
DEXA scan to assess bone density given chronic steroid use

imaging.MRCP: bile duct stricturing consistent with PSC

input.notes: Patient reports joint pain preceded current intestinal flare by 2 weeks. Erythema nodosum lesions are tender and have been present for 3 weeks. Previous uveitis episode was 8 months ago. Family history significant for mother with ulcerative colitis and PSC. Patient compliant with mesalamine but inadequate disease control. Discussed risks/benefits of biologic therapy extensively.

getFlareManagement.description: Retrieve flare management records for a patient

input.rescueTherapy: Prednisone 40mg daily for 5 days with taper over 2 weeks. If inadequate response within 72 hours, consider IV methylprednisolone 125mg daily x3 days. Topical clobetasol 0.05% BID for cutaneous lesions.
input.escalationCriteria: Failure to respond to oral steroids within 72 hours, worsening joint count >6 swollen joints, new organ involvement (renal, cardiac, pulmonary), CRP >50 mg/L despite therapy, inability to tolerate oral medications
input.admissionCriteria: Severe systemic symptoms with fever >38.5C, inability to perform ADLs, signs of organ dysfunction, need for IV therapy, failed outpatient management, social barriers to outpatient care
input.findings: 12 swollen joints (bilateral MCPs, PIPs, wrists, knees), morning stiffness >3 hours, unable to make fist bilaterally, effusion in both knees
input.assessment: Severe RA flare likely secondary to medication non-adherence (patient ran out of methotrexate 3 weeks ago). DAS28-CRP 6.8 indicating high disease activity. No evidence of infection on exam.
input.plan: Initiate rescue steroid therapy as above, restart methotrexate 20mg weekly, arrange follow-up in 1 week for joint aspiration if no improvement, patient education on medication adherence, consider biologic escalation if inadequate response

recommendations.text: Repeat CRP and ESR in 1 week
Follow-up rheumatology appointment
Physical therapy evaluation for joint protection techniques

results.labs: CRP 45 mg/L (elevated), ESR 62 mm/hr (elevated), RF 156 IU/mL
results.imaging: X-ray hands: progression of erosions in bilateral MCPs compared to 6 months ago

input.notes: Patient reports stress at work and financial difficulty obtaining medications. Social work consulted for medication assistance programs. Educated on importance of adherence and early intervention for future flares.

input.plan: Initiate R-CHOP chemotherapy cycle 1 on 1/22/2024. Restaging PET-CT after cycle 3. Monitor CBC, CMP, and LDH with each cycle. Cardiology clearance obtained given anthracycline use.

recommendations.text: 6 cycles R-CHOP chemotherapy
Interim PET-CT after cycle 3
MUGA scan or echocardiogram baseline and post-treatment
Fertility preservation counseling

results.ipiFactors: Age 62, LDH 520, ECOG 2, Stage III, 1 extranodal site

input.notes: Patient counseled extensively regarding diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment plan. Questions answered. Expressed understanding and agreement with recommended treatment approach. Social work and nutritional support referrals placed.

Update pneumococcal and influenza vaccines

getResponseAssessment.description: Retrieve response assessment records for a patient

input.bestResponse: Identify best overall response category: CR (Complete Response), PR (Partial Response), SD (Stable Disease), PD (Progressive Disease), often stated explicitly in assessment
input.depthOfResponse: Record percentage or quantitative measurement of tumor reduction (e.g., "70% reduction", "near complete response"), found in comparison statements
input.measurableDisease: Array of ALL target lesions with current measurements and locations, extract from imaging findings with anatomic locations and dimensions in cm
input.date: Date of the current assessment document being reviewed (report date, visit date, or encounter date)
input.type: Classification of assessment (e.g., "Baseline", "Restaging", "End of Treatment", "Surveillance", "Post-Surgery"), usually in report title or purpose
input.provider: Full name and specialty of physician documenting response assessment, from signature line or author field
input.facility: Complete name of institution or clinic where assessment performed, from header or letterhead
input.findings: Detailed narrative of imaging or clinical findings comparing current status to prior studies, extract entire findings paragraph with measurements
input.assessment: Clinical interpretation and response classification with supporting rationale, extract from "Assessment" or "Impression" section
input.plan: Treatment plan based on response (continue, modify, discontinue therapy), extract from "Plan" or "Recommendations" section
input.recommendations: Array of specific action items with associated dates, extract each discrete recommendation as separate array element with corresponding follow-up date
input.results: Object containing quantitative metrics (sum of target lesions, percent change from baseline, new lesion presence), extract numeric values from measurement tables
input.notes: Additional clinical context including toxicities, performance status, patient symptoms, quality of life comments, extracted from subjective or clinical notes sections
input.status: Current treatment status based on response (e.g., "Active - Responding", "Progressive - Treatment Change", "Complete - Surveillance"), infer from assessment and plan if not explicit

adverseEvents: Febrile neutropenia developed on day 10 of cycle
Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy in bilateral feet
doseModifications: Carboplatin reduced to 75% of original dose due to Grade 3 neutropenia
Paclitaxel held for this cycle secondary to neuropathy
treatmentDelays: Cycle 4 delayed by 7 days due to ANC <1500
input.findings: Patient reports improvement in nausea with current regimen. ANC recovered to 1800. Persistent numbness in toes bilaterally. No fever or signs of infection. ECOG performance status 1.
input.assessment: Grade 3 neutropenia resolved with growth factor support. Grade 2 chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy stable. Grade 2 nausea controlled with triple antiemetic therapy. Patient tolerating dose-reduced regimen.
input.plan: Continue carboplatin at 75% dose reduction. Hold paclitaxel for one additional cycle to allow neuropathy recovery. Reassess neuropathy symptoms prior to next treatment. Continue filgrastim prophylaxis.

recommendations.text: Neurology consultation if neuropathy worsens
Repeat CBC with differential in 1 week
Consider switching to non-platinum regimen if toxicities persist

input.notes: Patient education provided regarding neutropenic precautions. Instructed to report fever >100.4F immediately. Discussed long-term neuropathy risk and reversibility expectations. Patient verbalized understanding.

followUpSchedule.oncology: Every 3 months for year 1, every 6 months for years 2-5, annually thereafter
followUpSchedule.specialty: Cardiology evaluation annually due to anthracycline exposure

surveillanceTests.frequency: Every 3-6 months for 3 years, then annually

lateEffectsMonitoring.monitoring: Echocardiogram or MUGA scan at 6 months, then as clinically indicated
Arm circumference measurements, patient education on signs/symptoms
Clinical assessment of peripheral neuropathy from taxane therapy

healthMaintenance.vaccinations: Annual influenza, COVID-19 boosters, pneumococcal per guidelines
healthMaintenance.screening: Age-appropriate colorectal, cervical cancer screening; bone density screening given aromatase inhibitor use
healthMaintenance.lifestyle: Weight management, regular exercise (150 min/week), Mediterranean diet, alcohol limitation

recurrenceSigns: New persistent breast mass or skin changes
Bone pain not relieved by rest or conservative measures
Persistent cough, dyspnea, or chest pain
Abdominal pain, bloating, or changes in bowel habits
Severe headaches, visual changes, or neurological symptoms
Unexplained weight loss greater than 10 pounds
input.surveillanceStrategy: No routine CT/PET/bone scans in absence of symptoms per ASCO/NCCN guidelines. Surveillance based on clinical examination, symptom monitoring, and annual mammography. Imaging only if symptomatic or abnormal exam findings.

psychosocialSupport.resources: Referral to survivorship support group, LIVESTRONG navigation services
psychosocialSupport.screening: Distress thermometer score 6/10 - referred to psycho-oncology
psychosocialSupport.concerns: Anxiety about recurrence, body image concerns post-mastectomy, cognitive changes
psychosocialSupport.interventions: Individual counseling sessions, survivorship workshop enrollment

input.findings: Patient completed ACT chemotherapy (dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide followed by paclitaxel) and left mastectomy with sentinel node biopsy. Stage IIA (T2N0M0) ER+/PR+/HER2- invasive ductal carcinoma. Received 6 weeks radiation therapy to chest wall. Currently on anastrozole.
input.assessment: 34-year-old female with excellent response to treatment, no evidence of disease 6 months post-treatment completion. Managing aromatase inhibitor-related arthralgias. Mild fatigue and chemotherapy-related cognitive changes improving. At risk for long-term cardiac effects from anthracyclines and potential for osteoporosis on aromatase inhibitor.
input.plan: Continue anastrozole for total 5-10 years. Close surveillance with clinical exams and annual imaging. Monitor for late effects including cardiotoxicity, bone health, and menopausal symptoms. Coordinate care with primary care physician for preventive health maintenance. Address psychosocial needs and optimize quality of life.

recommendations.recommendation: Continue anastrozole 1 mg daily
Baseline bone density scan (DEXA) within 3 months
Calcium 1200 mg and Vitamin D 2000 IU daily supplementation
Genetic counseling given young age at diagnosis
Physical therapy evaluation for lymphedema prevention and exercise program

results.lastMammogram: 2024-01-10 - BI-RADS 2, benign findings, recommend annual follow-up
results.imaging: No baseline CT chest/abdomen/pelvis per guideline-concordant care

input.notes: Patient educated on signs/symptoms of recurrence and importance of reporting new concerning symptoms promptly. Provided written survivorship care plan. Discussed fertility preservation options (completed prior to treatment). Copy of care plan sent to primary care physician Dr. James Miller. Patient verbalized understanding and agreement with surveillance plan.

Establish care with high-risk breast clinic
Follow-up for genetic test results

results.interpretation: Positive for hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome
results.penetrance: Lifetime breast cancer risk 55-72%, ovarian cancer risk 39-44%

input.notes: Patient expressed relief at having concrete information to guide decision-making. Discussed cascade testing for sister and daughters when age-appropriate. Avoid dairy products, calcium supplements, and antacids within 2 hours of dose
Increase dietary iron intake through red meat, spinach, fortified cereals
Follow-up CBC and iron panel
Gynecology referral for menorrhagia management

input.results: Object containing outcomes of the referral such as whether patient was contacted, attended sessions, engagement level, or facilitator feedback. Extract any documented follow-up information as key-value pairs.
input.notes: Additional contextual information including patient preferences (language, schedule, location preferences), barriers to attendance, transportation availability, or special accommodations needed.
input.status: Current state of referral (e.g., "Pending", "Active - Attending", "Completed Intake", "Declined", "Waitlisted", "Discontinued", "Transferred"). Extract most recent status update.
