getPolypharmacy.description: Retrieve polypharmacy records for a patient

input.provider: Dr. Sarah Chen, PharmD, Clinical Pharmacist
input.findings: Patient currently taking 14 medications. Identified duplicate therapy (lisinopril 10mg and enalapril 5mg both prescribed). Potential drug-drug interaction between warfarin and aspirin 325mg increasing bleeding risk. Three medications without clear indication: alprazolam 0.5mg BID, pantoprazole 40mg daily beyond recommended duration, diphenhydramine 25mg HS. Adherence concerns noted with complex dosing schedules.
input.assessment: High-risk polypharmacy with 14 concurrent medications. Medication-related problems identified: therapeutic duplication of ACE inhibitors, inappropriate use of benzodiazepine in elderly patient (Beers Criteria), overuse of PPI without gastroprotection indication, anticholinergic burden score of 4 (high risk). Patient experiencing dizziness likely related to orthostatic hypotension from multiple antihypertensives.
input.plan: Discontinue enalapril 5mg (duplicate ACE inhibitor). Taper and discontinue alprazolam over 4 weeks given fall risk and cognitive concerns. Stop pantoprazole after 2-week taper. Reduce aspirin to 81mg daily. Monitor INR closely after aspirin dose reduction. Replace diphenhydramine with non-anticholinergic sleep aid. Schedule follow-up in 2 weeks to assess blood pressure control and symptom resolution.

recommendations.recommendation: Discontinue enalapril 5mg immediately - duplicate ACE inhibitor therapy
Begin alprazolam taper: reduce to 0.25mg BID for 2 weeks, then 0.25mg daily for 2 weeks, then discontinue
Reduce pantoprazole to 20mg daily for 1 week then discontinue
Change aspirin from 325mg to 81mg daily
Check INR 3 days after aspirin dose reduction
Replace diphenhydramine with melatonin 3mg at bedtime
Follow-up appointment to reassess blood pressure and dizziness symptoms

input.notes: Patient's daughter present during consultation and agrees with deprescribing plan. Patient reports filling prescriptions at two different pharmacies which contributed to lack of interaction screening. Educated on importance of using single pharmacy. Provided written medication schedule with changes highlighted. Will coordinate with PCP Dr. Martinez regarding medication changes and monitoring plan.

getMedicationDeprescribing.description: Retrieve medication deprescribing records for a patient

input.findings: Patient taking 14 medications daily. Identified duplicate therapy with omeprazole 40mg and famotidine 20mg. Patient reports dizziness and falls (3 in past month). Benzodiazepine use (lorazepam 1mg BID) for 8 years without clear indication. Glyburide use in 82-year-old with HbA1c 6.2% poses hypoglycemia risk.
input.assessment: High-risk polypharmacy with potentially inappropriate medications per Beers Criteria. Falls likely related to benzodiazepine and antihypertensive overtreatment (BP averaging 105/62). Diabetes overtreatment in elderly patient. PPI use without documented indication beyond 6 months.
input.plan: Initiate systematic deprescribing protocol. Taper lorazepam 0.5mg every 2 weeks. Discontinue glyburide, monitor fingersticks. Reduce lisinopril from 20mg to 10mg. Discontinue famotidine (duplicate therapy). Attempt PPI discontinuation after stepping down to omeprazole 20mg daily for 2 weeks.

recommendations.text: Discontinue famotidine 20mg immediately due to duplication with omeprazole
Begin lorazepam taper: reduce to 0.5mg BID for 2 weeks, then 0.5mg daily for 2 weeks, then discontinue
Discontinue glyburide 5mg, monitor blood glucose twice daily for 1 week
Reduce lisinopril to 10mg daily, recheck BP in 1 week
Reduce omeprazole to 20mg daily for 2 weeks, then attempt discontinuation if no GERD symptoms

input.notes: Patient's daughter present and supportive of deprescribing. Patient expressed relief at taking fewer medications. Educated on potential withdrawal symptoms from benzodiazepine taper. Provided written schedule for medication changes. Home health contacted to monitor BP and blood glucose during transition.

input.findings: PSA level elevated at 5.8 ng/mL, up from 3.2 ng/mL six months ago. Digital rectal exam reveals mild prostatic enlargement without nodules or induration. Patient reports mild urinary hesitancy but denies hematuria, dysuria, or significant nocturia.
input.assessment: Elevated PSA with rapid increase over 6-month period. Differential includes benign prostatic hyperplasia, prostatitis, or possible malignancy. Age-appropriate screening warranted given family history of prostate cancer (father diagnosed at age 68).
input.plan: Refer to urology for further evaluation. Repeat PSA in 6 weeks after treatment for possible prostatitis. Prescribe doxycycline 100mg BID for 14 days. Order free PSA and PSA velocity calculation. Discuss potential for prostate biopsy if PSA remains elevated.

recommendations.recommendation: Urology referral for elevated PSA evaluation
Repeat PSA testing after antibiotic course
Annual PSA screening to continue given risk factors

input.notes: Patient counseled on significance of PSA elevation and importance of urology follow-up. Discussed risks and benefits of prostate biopsy. Patient expressed concerns about false positives but agrees with plan. Wife present during discussion and supportive of workup.

bloodSmear.morphology: Sickle cells present, target cells, polychromasia

bloodSmear.interpretation: Consistent with sickle cell disease with evidence of hemolysis

boneMarrow.findings: Erythroid hyperplasia consistent with chronic hemolysis

input.stagingClassification: HbSS genotype, severe phenotype with frequent vaso-occlusive crises

treatmentPlan.targetResponse: Increase HbF to >20%, reduce crisis frequency

chemotherapy.indication: Disease-modifying therapy for sickle cell disease

supportiveCare: Penicillin VK 500 mg twice daily prophylaxis
Pain management protocol with opioid agreement
input.transfusionSupport: PRBCs for Hgb <6 or symptomatic anemia, phenotypically matched units due to alloantibodies
input.transplantEligibility: Potential transplant candidate - HLA typing ordered for patient and siblings, referral to transplant center for evaluation

clinicalTrials.trialName: Gene Therapy for Sickle Cell Disease (NCT12345678)
clinicalTrials.eligibility: Eligible - meets age and disease severity criteria
clinicalTrials.status: Discussed with patient, information packet provided

prognosis.overallPrognosis: Guarded - severe phenotype with multiple complications
prognosis.riskFactification: High risk based on frequent hospitalizations and organ damage
prognosis.lifeExpectancy: Reduced compared to general population without transplant

input.followUp: Return to clinic in 4 weeks for CBC, reticulocyte count, and hydroxyurea tolerance assessment. Transplant evaluation scheduled for March 2024
input.findings: 28-year-old female with HbSS sickle cell disease presenting with 6 vaso-occlusive crises in past year requiring hospitalization. CBC shows Hgb 7.2, WBC 12.4, platelets 385K, reticulocyte count 12%. LDH elevated at 456, total bilirubin 3.2 (indirect 2.8). Peripheral smear with numerous sickle cells and target cells.
input.assessment: Severe sickle cell disease (HbSS) with frequent vaso-occlusive episodes despite conservative management. Evidence of chronic hemolysis and functional asplenia. Patient has developed alloantibodies complicating transfusion management. Current management inadequate for disease control.
input.plan: 1) Initiate hydroxyurea therapy with goal to increase HbF and reduce crisis frequency. 2) Continue prophylactic penicillin and folic acid. 3) Pursue HLA typing for transplant evaluation. 4) Screen for silent cerebral infarcts with brain MRI/MRA. 5) Ophthalmology referral for retinopathy screening. 6) Discuss gene therapy clinical trial enrollment.

recommendations.recommendation: Start hydroxyurea 1000 mg daily
Obtain echocardiogram to assess for pulmonary hypertension
Schedule transcranial Doppler ultrasound

input.notes: Patient very motivated for disease-modifying therapy. Expressed interest in both transplant and gene therapy options. Family history significant for sibling with sickle cell disease who died at age 24 from acute chest syndrome. Patient has had two episodes of acute chest syndrome requiring ICU admission.

getOperativeReportDetails.description: Retrieve operative report details records for a patient

input.indication: Acute cholecystitis with cholelithiasis, recurrent biliary colic refractory to conservative management
input.postoperativeDiagnosis: Acute calculous cholecystitis with gallstones, mild adhesions
input.procedureDetails: Patient positioned supine under general anesthesia. Pneumoperitoneum established with Veress needle at umbilicus. Four-port technique utilized with 10mm umbilical camera port and three 5mm working ports. Gallbladder dissected from liver bed using electrocautery. Cystic duct and artery identified and clipped. Intraoperative cholangiogram performed showing patent common bile duct. Specimen removed through umbilical port in endocatch bag.
input.findings: Distended gallbladder with multiple stones, mild inflammatory adhesions to omentum, patent cystic duct and common bile duct on cholangiogram, normal liver appearance
input.notes: Patient tolerated procedure well. All sponge and instrument counts correct. Fascia closed with 0-Vicryl suture. Skin approximated with dermabond. Patient to recovery in stable condition.

input.goalsOfCare: Patient states primary goal is to remain comfortable at home with family for as long as possible. Wants to avoid hospitalizations unless absolutely necessary for symptom management. Hopes to attend granddaughter's wedding in April if health permits.
input.values: Patient values independence, dignity, and being surrounded by family. Religious faith important - wants ability to attend church services. Prioritizes quality time over quantity. Does not want to be a burden on family but wants to remain at home.
input.codeStatus: DNR/DNI - Do Not Resuscitate, Do Not Intubate. Patient clearly stated no CPR, no mechanical ventilation. Comfortable with medical interventions for symptom management but no life-prolonging measures.
input.treatmentPreferences: Willing to accept blood transfusions and IV antibiotics for comfort. Declines chemotherapy, dialysis, or artificial nutrition/hydration. Wants aggressive pain and symptom management. Prefers oral medications when possible. Open to hospice enrollment when appropriate.
input.healthcareAgent: Maria Martinez (daughter) - primary healthcare proxy. Contact: 555-234-5678. Secondary agent: Carlos Martinez (son) 555-876-5432. MOLST form completed and signed.
input.advanceDirectiveStatus: Completed and signed advance directive on file as of 01/15/2024. Copy provided to patient, healthcare proxy, and PCP. Uploaded to EHR. State POLST form completed. Living will executed 2019, reviewed and reaffirmed today.
input.prognosisDiscussion: Discussed stage IV pancreatic cancer with estimated prognosis of 3-6 months. Patient aware of terminal diagnosis. Discussed expected disease trajectory including potential complications: increasing pain, jaundice, weight loss, fatigue. Patient expressed understanding and acceptance.
input.qualityOfLife: Patient defines quality of life as being alert enough to recognize family, minimal pain, ability to participate in conversations, and maintaining some independence with ADLs. Stated would not want life prolonged if bedbound, unable to communicate, or experiencing uncontrolled pain.
input.spiritualConcerns: Catholic faith central to patient's life. Wants Last Rites when appropriate. Desires weekly visits from parish priest. Finds comfort in rosary prayers. Daughter will coordinate with St. Anthony's Church. No specific concerns about afterlife - expresses peace with death as part of God's plan.
input.followUpPlanned: Palliative care follow-up in 2 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen. Will reassess goals and hospice eligibility at next visit. Family meeting scheduled for 02/01/2024 to include all children. Social work to assist with advance directive copies distribution.
input.provider: Dr. Sarah Chen, Palliative Care Medicine
input.notes: 90-minute discussion with patient fully alert and participatory. Daughter tearful but supportive. Patient demonstrated clear decision-making capacity. Used teach-back method to confirm understanding. Provided written materials about hospice services and symptom management. Patient expressed relief at having these conversations and making wishes known.

getHepatitisCHistory.description: Retrieve hepatitis c history records for a patient

input.viralLoad: Historical range 850,000 to 4.2 million IU/mL (2015-2018). Currently undetectable post-SVR.
previousTreatments: 2016: Sofosbuvir 400mg + Ribavirin 1200mg daily x 24 weeks (relapse at post-treatment week 4)
treatmentOutcomes: First treatment (2016): EOT response but relapse - HCV RNA undetectable at week 24, rebounded to 1.8M IU/mL at post-treatment week 4
Second treatment (2019): SVR12 achieved - HCV RNA undetectable at 12 weeks post-treatment, sustained cure confirmed
sideEffects: 2016 treatment: Severe ribavirin-related hemolytic anemia (Hgb dropped from 14.2 to 9.8 g/dL), required dose reduction from 1200mg to 800mg at week 8. Persistent fatigue, headaches, irritability throughout treatment.
2019 treatment: Minimal side effects - mild headache first week only, otherwise well-tolerated. No anemia.
input.complianceIssues: Excellent adherence to 2019 regimen (100% self-reported, confirmed by pharmacy refills). 2016 treatment complicated by insurance lapse month 4-5 (missed 6 weeks of therapy due to coverage gap, restarted after Medicaid approved), may have contributed to relapse. Used pill organizer and smartphone reminders for 2019 treatment. No missed doses documented.
input.relapseHistory: Relapsed 4 weeks after completing first treatment course (2016). HCV RNA rebounded to 1.8 million IU/mL from undetectable. Likely multifactorial: genotype 3 (higher relapse risk with SOF+RBV), treatment interruption during month 4-5 due to insurance issues, baseline F3 fibrosis. No relapse after 2019 treatment - SVR sustained >5 years.

coinfections.hbv: Immune - HBsAb positive (vaccinated 2015), HBsAg negative, HBcAb negative

input.liverBiopsy: Percutaneous liver biopsy performed May 2015 (before first treatment): METAVIR score F3 (advanced fibrosis with bridging septa, no cirrhosis), Activity Grade A2 (moderate inflammation). Portal and periportal fibrosis with architectural distortion but intact lobular structure. Moderate lymphocytic infiltration. No steatosis. No iron overload. Conclusion: Chronic hepatitis C with advanced fibrosis (F3), consistent with 15-20 year disease duration based on histology.
input.fibrosisStage: Baseline (2015): F3 by biopsy. Post-SVR monitoring (2021): FibroScan 9.1 kPa (F2-F3 borderline), improved from pre-treatment 13.2 kPa. Most recent (2024): FibroScan 7.8 kPa (F2), demonstrating fibrosis regression after viral cure. Platelet count improved 142K (2015) to 178K (2024).
input.cirrhosisHistory: Never developed cirrhosis. Baseline F3 fibrosis in 2015 with high risk of progression to F4, but achieved viral cure before cirrhosis onset. Post-SVR fibrosis regression documented on serial elastography. Current F2 stage represents significant improvement. No evidence of portal hypertension (normal platelets, no varices on 2020 EGD screening).
input.hepatocellularCarcinoma: No history of HCC. AFP monitored semi-annually 2015-2020 due to F3 fibrosis, always <5 ng/mL (normal). Liver ultrasound surveillance discontinued 2021 after confirming F2 regression (not meeting F4 criteria for HCC screening). No focal liver lesions ever identified. AFP most recent 3.2 ng/mL (2024).
recommendations: Annual hepatology follow-up with FibroScan and liver panel
Lifelong alcohol abstinence (critical given F3 history)
Diabetes control: A1C target <7%, minimize metabolic liver stress
Weight management: Mediterranean diet, regular exercise
Substance use recovery: Continue NA attendance, counseling support
Hepatitis A/B immunity: Verify titers, booster if needed
Avoid hepatotoxic medications/supplements (discuss all new meds with provider)
Harm reduction: Safe injection practices if any future medical injections
Monitor for symptoms: Jaundice, abdominal swelling, confusion, bleeding (unlikely but report immediately)
Educate family: HCV is curable, patient is non-infectious, no household transmission risk

results.liver_biopsy_2015: METAVIR F3A2 - advanced fibrosis, moderate activity

input.status: Cured - SVR60+ sustained, fibrosis regression F3→F2, annual monitoring
